title: Syllabus
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of this workshop, participants will be able to:

Objective 1 — e.g. describe the biology and measurement process underlying single cell RNA-seq data.
Objective 2 — e.g. perform quality control, filtering, and normalization on a scRNA-seq count matrix.
Objective 3 — e.g. carry out dimensionality reduction and clustering, and interpret the results.
Objective 4 — e.g. identify marker genes and annotate cell types.
Objective 5 — e.g. perform differential expression between groups of cells.
Objective 6 — e.g. integrate multiple datasets and correct for batch effects.

title: Appendix E - Git, GitHub & Version Control
text:
Overview







Pre-requisite Material


This appendix introduces Git and GitHub — essential tools for version control and collaboration in scientific computing. You will learn how to track changes in your code, collaborate with others, and host your work online. These skills are critical for reproducible research.

title: Appendix E - Git, GitHub & Version Control
text:
Git and GitHub
What is Git?

Git is a distributed version control system
Tracks changes in your code over time
Allows collaboration with others
Maintains complete history of all changes

What is GitHub?

GitHub is a web-based hosting service for Git repositories
Provides cloud storage for your code
Enables collaboration features
Hosts documentation and websites (GitHub Pages)
Free for public repositories

## Install and Configure Git {.smaller}

::: panel-tabset
### Install

``` bash
# Check if Git is installed
git --version

# macOS (with Homebrew)
brew install git

# Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt-get install git

# Windows: Download from git-scm.com
Configure
# Set your name and email
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@example.com"

# Check your settings

Interpretation

Your name and email are attached to every commit you make
Use your university email for academic work

text:
Initialize a Repository

Create NewClone ExistingInterpretation


# Create a new repository
mkdir my_project
cd my_project
git init


# Clone an existing repository from GitHub
git clone https://github.com/username/repository.git



git init creates a new Git repository in the current directory
git clone downloads an existing repository from GitHub
Cloning creates a local copy with the complete history
Only do git init once per project

title: Appendix E - Git, GitHub & Version Control
text:
Track Changes

CommandsInterpretation


# Check status --- what has changed?
git status

# Add files to staging area
git add filename.txt        # Add specific file
git add .                   # Add all changes

# Commit changes (save a snapshot)
git commit -m "Descriptive message about changes"



git status shows you what files have been modified, added, or deleted
git add stages changes — prepares them to be committed
git commit creates a permanent snapshot with a descriptive message
Think of staging as selecting which photos to put in an album, and committing as actually printing the album

title: Appendix E - Git, GitHub & Version Control
text:
Working with Remotes

CommandsInterpretation


# Add a remote repository
git remote add origin https://github.com/username/repo.git

# View remotes
git remote -v

# Push changes to GitHub
git push origin main

# Pull changes from GitHub
git pull origin main

# Fetch without merging
git fetch origin



origin is the conventional name for your primary remote (GitHub)
push sends your local commits to GitHub
pull downloads and merges remote changes to your local copy
fetch downloads changes but doesn’t merge (safer for inspection)
Always pull before you start working to get the latest changes

title: Appendix E - Git, GitHub & Version Control
text:
Branching and Merging

VisualCommandsInterpretation





Git Branching and Merging





# Create and switch to new branch
git checkout -b feature-branch

# Switch branches
git checkout main

# Merge branch into current branch
git merge feature-branch

# Delete branch after merging
git branch -d feature-branch



Branches allow you to work on features independently
The main branch should always contain working code
Create feature branches for new work, then merge when ready
This protects your main code from incomplete changes

title: Appendix E - Git, GitHub & Version Control
text:
Common Git Workflow

Daily CycleBest Practices


# 1. Start your day - update your local copy
git pull origin main

# 2. Create a feature branch
git checkout -b new-feature

# 3. Make changes and commit regularly
git add .
git commit -m "Add new analysis function"

# 4. Push your branch
git push origin new-feature

# 5. Create pull request on GitHub (via website)


Commit Messages:

Be descriptive and concise
Use present tense: “Add feature” not “Added feature”
Reference issues: “Fix bug #123”

Repository Organization:
project/
├── README.md          # Project description
├── data/              # Raw data (consider .gitignore)
├── analysis/          # Analysis scripts
├── results/           # Output files
└── docs/              # Documentation

title: Appendix E - Git, GitHub & Version Control
text:
Viewing Commit History

CommandsInterpretation


# View commit history
git log

# Prettier one-line format
git log --oneline

# With graph visualization
git log --graph --oneline --all

# Show changes in commits
git log -p

# Show last n commits
git log -3



git log shows the complete history of commits
Each commit has a unique hash, author, date, and message
--oneline gives a compact view for quick scanning
--graph shows branch structure visually in the terminal

title: Appendix E - Git, GitHub & Version Control
text:
.gitignore File

PurposeCommon PatternsInterpretation


Exclude files from version control:
# Create .gitignore file
touch .gitignore


# Data files (too large for Git)
*.fastq
*.fasta
*.bam
*.vcf
data/raw/*

# R files
.Rhistory
.RData
.Rproj.user

# Output files
results/temp/*
*.log

# System files
.DS_Store



.gitignore tells Git which files to never track
Put large data files, temporary files, and system files here
This keeps your repository clean and small
Sensitive files (passwords, API keys) should always be in .gitignore

title: Appendix F - High-Performance Computing with Talapas
text:
Overview







Pre-requisite Material


This appendix covers the University of Oregon’s high-performance computing (HPC) cluster, Talapas. You will learn how to access the cluster, submit jobs using SLURM, manage software modules, and run GPU-accelerated computations. See Appendix A for background on cluster computing concepts.

title: Appendix F - High-Performance Computing with Talapas
text:
Talapas Overview

Talapas is UO’s high-performance computing (HPC) cluster
Named from Chinook word for coyote (educator and keeper of knowledge)
Managed by Research Advanced Computing Services (RACS)
Located in the Allen Hall Data Center
Documentation: uoracs.github.io/talapas2-knowledge-base

Key Resources:

Over 14,520 CPU cores
129 TB of memory
89 GPUs (including A100, H100, V100)
3+ Petabytes of storage

# Accessing Talapas {background-color="#2c3e50"}

## Login Nodes vs Compute Nodes {.smaller}

::: panel-tabset
### Login Nodes

-   Where you land when you SSH into Talapas
-   For **light tasks only**: file management, editing, job submission
-   Shared by all users --- be considerate!
-   Heavy processes will be killed

### Compute Nodes

-   Where your actual work runs
-   Accessed through the SLURM scheduler
-   Different types for different needs:
    -   Standard CPU nodes
    -   GPU nodes
    -   High-memory ("fat") nodes
:::

## Node Types {.smaller}

| Node Type      | Cores   | Memory     | Purpose                |
|----------------|---------|------------|------------------------|
| Standard CPU   | 28--128 | 128--512 GB| General computation    |
| Large Memory   | 56      | 1--4 TB    | Memory-intensive work  |
| GPU (K80/A100) | 28--48  | 256--512 GB| Machine learning, CUDA |
| Interactive    | Various | Various    | Testing, development   |

### Partitions (Queues):

-   `compute` --- General purpose CPU partition (default)
-   `short` --- Jobs up to 24 hours
-   `long` --- Jobs up to 14 days
-   `gpu` / `longgpu` --- GPU-enabled nodes
-   `fat` / `longfat` --- High-memory nodes
-   `preempt` --- Access to all nodes (may be interrupted)

## Getting Access {.smaller}

::: panel-tabset
### Step 1: Join a PIRG

-   **PIRG** = Principal Investigator Research Group
-   Contact your PI to be added to their PIRG
-   Or contact RACS to create a new PIRG

### Step 2: Request Access

-   Visit [racs.uoregon.edu/request-access](https://racs.uoregon.edu/request-access)
-   Your username = your Duck ID
-   Your password = your UO password
:::

## Logging In {.smaller}

::: panel-tabset
### Web Browser

[ondemand.talapas.uoregon.edu](https://ondemand.talapas.uoregon.edu)

-   No command line needed!
-   File browser for uploads/downloads
-   Interactive applications: JupyterLab, RStudio, MATLAB, Remote desktop
-   Great for beginners and quick data analysis

### Command Line

``` bash
# Basic SSH connection
ssh yourduckid@login.talapas.uoregon.edu

# Or specific login node
ssh yourduckid@login1.talapas.uoregon.edu
From Windows: Use PuTTY, MobaXterm, or Windows Terminal
Off Campus? First connect to UO VPN, then SSH as normal :::

### Quotas

| Location                | Quota                 | Purpose                     |
|-------------------------|-----------------------|-----------------------------|
| `/home/duckid`          | 250 GB                | Config files, small scripts |
| `/projects/PIRG/duckid` | Shared (2 TB default) | Large data, analysis        |

| `/tmp`                  | Local to node         | Temporary scratch space     |

### Important Notes

-   **Data is NOT automatically backed up!**

-   Clean up when done

# Check your storage usage

title: Appendix F - High-Performance Computing with Talapas
text:
What is SLURM?

Simple Linux Utility for Resource Management
Job scheduler and workload manager
Allocates resources fairly among users
Ensures efficient cluster utilization

Description
Example

Queue to use

title: Appendix C - GREP, Regular Expressions & Shell Scripting
text:
Overview







Pre-requisite Material


This appendix covers two essential Unix skills: (1) using GREP and regular expressions for powerful pattern matching in text files, and (2) writing shell scripts to automate repetitive tasks. These are critical skills for working with large datasets in bioinformatics and bioengineering.

title: Appendix C - GREP, Regular Expressions & Shell Scripting
text:
What is GREP?

GREP = Global Regular Expression Print
Powerful text search tool in Unix
Searches for patterns in files or input streams
Returns lines that match the pattern
Essential for large data processing

Basic syntax:
grep [options] pattern [file...]

title: Appendix C - GREP, Regular Expressions & Shell Scripting
text:
Basic GREP Usage

Common OptionsInterpretation


# Search for a simple pattern
grep "ACGT" sequences.txt

# Case-insensitive search
grep -i "gene" annotations.txt

# Count matching lines
grep -c ">" proteins.fasta

# Show line numbers
grep -n "ERROR" logfile.txt

# Invert match (show non-matching lines)
grep -v "^#" data.txt    # Skip comment lines

# Search multiple files
grep "mutation" *.txt



-i ignores case — useful when you’re unsure of capitalization
-c returns just the count, not the matching lines
-n shows line numbers — helpful for locating matches in large files
-v inverts the match — essential for filtering out headers or comments
Searching multiple files with wildcards saves time

title: Appendix C - GREP, Regular Expressions & Shell Scripting
text:
Introduction to Regular Expressions

MetacharactersExamples


Regular expressions (regex) are patterns for matching text:

. = any single character
* = zero or more of preceding character
+ = one or more of preceding character
? = zero or one of preceding character
^ = start of line
$ = end of line
[abc] = any character in set
[^abc] = any character NOT in set
\ = escape special characters



# Match DNA sequences
grep "^ATG" genes.txt         # Lines starting with ATG
grep "TAG$" genes.txt         # Lines ending with TAG
grep "A.G" sequences.txt      # A, any char, then G

# Character classes
grep "[ACGT]" dna.txt         # Any DNA base
grep "[^ACGT]" dna.txt        # Non-DNA characters
grep "[0-9]" data.txt         # Any digit

# Quantifiers
grep "A\{3\}" sequence.txt    # Exactly 3 A's
grep "T\{2,4\}" sequence.txt  # 2 to 4 T's
grep "GC*" sequence.txt       # G followed by 0+ C's

title: Appendix C - GREP, Regular Expressions & Shell Scripting
text:
Extended Regular Expressions

SyntaxCombining with Pipes


Use grep -E or egrep for extended regex:
# Alternation (OR)
grep -E "start|stop|pause" commands.txt

# Grouping
grep -E "(ATG|GTG|TTG)" codons.txt

# Plus quantifier (one or more)
grep -E "A+T+G+" sequences.txt

# Question mark (optional)
grep -E "colou?r" british_american.txt

# Complex patterns for bioinformatics
grep -E "^>sp\|[A-Z0-9]+\|" uniprot.fasta  # UniProt headers
grep -E "[ACGT]{20,}" primers.txt          # Sequences 20bp+


# Count unique sequence headers
grep "^>" sequences.fasta | sort | uniq | wc -l

# Extract gene names from GFF file
grep -v "^#" annotation.gff | cut -f9 | grep -oE "gene=[^;]+"

# Find sequences without stop codons
grep -v -E "(TAA|TAG|TGA)" orfs.txt

# Search and replace with sed (uses regex)
grep "gene" data.txt | sed 's/gene/GENE/g'

title: Appendix C - GREP, Regular Expressions & Shell Scripting
text:
Analyzing Sequence Content with GREP

Base CountingPattern Finding


# Count total bases in a genome
grep -v "^>" human_genome.fa | tr -d '\n' | wc -c

# Count each type of base
grep -v "^>" human_genome.fa | tr -d '\n' |
  fold -w1 | sort | uniq -c

# Calculate GC content
grep -v "^>" human_genome.fa | tr -d '\n' |
  tr -cd 'GCgc' | wc -c


# Find all instances of a restriction enzyme site (EcoRI: GAATTC)
grep -v "^>" human_genome.fa | \
  tr -d '\n' | \
  grep -o "GAATTC" | \
  wc -l

# Find simple sequence repeats (e.g., "ATATAT...")
grep -v "^>" human_genome.fa | grep -oE "(AT){5,}"

# Find all start codons and their positions
grep -v "^>" human_genome.fa | \
  tr -d '\n' | \
  grep -b -o "ATG" | \
  head -20

title: Appendix C - GREP, Regular Expressions & Shell Scripting
text:
What is a Shell Script?

Text file containing Unix commands
Automates repetitive tasks
Makes complex workflows reproducible
Essential for bioinformatics pipelines
Can include:

Variables and arrays
Conditionals (if/then/else)
Loops (for/while)
Functions
Command-line arguments

title: Appendix C - GREP, Regular Expressions & Shell Scripting
text:
Creating Your First Script

CodeHow to RunInterpretation


#!/bin/bash
# This is a shebang - tells system which interpreter to use

# This is a comment - documents your code
echo "Hello, Bioengineering!"

# Set a variable
NAME="DNA Analysis"
echo "Running $NAME"

# Use command output as variable
DATE=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)
echo "Analysis date: $DATE"

# Simple calculation
COUNT=5
DOUBLE=$((COUNT * 2))
echo "Double of $COUNT is $DOUBLE"


# Save as first_script.sh, then:

# Option 1: Run with bash
bash first_script.sh

# Option 2: Make executable first
chmod +x first_script.sh
./first_script.sh



The #!/bin/bash shebang tells the system which interpreter to use
Comments with # document your code
Variables are assigned with = (no spaces around =)
$() captures command output as a variable
$(()) performs arithmetic

title: Appendix C - GREP, Regular Expressions & Shell Scripting
text:
Command-Line Arguments

CodeUsageInterpretation


#!/bin/bash
# Script: process_fasta.sh

echo "Script name: $0"       # $0 is the script name
echo "Number of arguments: $#"

# Check if enough arguments provided
if [ $# -lt 2 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 input.fasta output.txt"
    exit 1
fi

echo "Processing file: $1"   # First argument
echo "Output will be: $2"    # Second argument
echo "All arguments: $@"     # All arguments


# Run as:
./process_fasta.sh input.fa results.txt



$0 = script name, $1 = first argument, $2 = second, etc.
$# = total number of arguments
$@ = all arguments as a list
Always validate arguments before using them

title: Appendix C - GREP, Regular Expressions & Shell Scripting
text:
Variables and Arrays

CodeInterpretation


#!/bin/bash

# Variables (no spaces around =)
SPECIES="Homo sapiens"
CHROMOSOME=21
GENE_COUNT=234

# Arrays
BASES=(A C G T)
echo "First base: ${BASES[0]}"
echo "All bases: ${BASES[@]}"

# Add to array
BASES+=(N)

# Array length
echo "Number of bases: ${#BASES[@]}"

# String operations
FILE="sample_001.fastq.gz"
NAME="${FILE%.fastq.gz}"     # Remove suffix
echo "Sample name: $NAME"



Variables: no spaces around =, reference with $
Arrays: created with (), indexed with ${arr[i]}
${#arr[@]} gives array length
${var%pattern} removes suffix pattern from variable

title: Appendix C - GREP, Regular Expressions & Shell Scripting
text:
Conditionals (if/then/else)

CodeOperators


#!/bin/bash

# Numeric comparisons
COUNT=100
if [ $COUNT -gt 50 ]; then
    echo "High read count"
elif [ $COUNT -gt 10 ]; then
    echo "Medium read count"
else
    echo "Low read count"
fi

# String comparisons
FILETYPE="fasta"
if [ "$FILETYPE" = "fasta" ]; then
    echo "Processing FASTA file"
fi

# File tests
if [ -f "data.txt" ]; then
    echo "File exists"
fi

if [ -d "results" ]; then
    echo "Directory exists"
else
    mkdir results
fi





Operator
Meaning




-gt
Greater than


-lt
Less than


-eq
Equal to


-ne
Not equal to


-ge
Greater than or equal


-le
Less than or equal


-f
File exists


-d
Directory exists


=
String equality


!=
String inequality

title: Appendix C - GREP, Regular Expressions & Shell Scripting
text:
Loops

For LoopsWhile LoopsInterpretation


#!/bin/bash

# For loop over list
for BASE in A C G T; do
    echo "Processing base: $BASE"
done

# For loop over files
for FILE in *.fasta; do
    echo "Analyzing $FILE"
    grep -c ">" "$FILE"
done

# For loop with counter
for i in {1..10}; do
    echo "Iteration $i"
done


#!/bin/bash

# While loop with counter
COUNT=1
while [ $COUNT -le 5 ]; do
    echo "Count: $COUNT"
    COUNT=$((COUNT + 1))
done

# Read file line by line
while read LINE; do
    echo "Processing: $LINE"
done < input.txt



for loops iterate over a list of items (words, files, numbers)
while loops continue as long as a condition is true
Reading files line-by-line with while read is very common
Always use "$FILE" in quotes to handle filenames with spaces

title: Appendix C - GREP, Regular Expressions & Shell Scripting
text:
Functions

CodeInterpretation


#!/bin/bash

# Define a function
count_sequences() {
    local FILE=$1  # Local variable
    local COUNT=$(grep -c "^>" "$FILE")
    echo "File $FILE has $COUNT sequences"
    return $COUNT
}

# Function with multiple parameters
process_fasta() {
    local INPUT=$1
    local OUTPUT=$2

    echo "Processing $INPUT..."
    grep "^>" "$INPUT" > "$OUTPUT"
    count_sequences "$INPUT"
}

# Call functions
count_sequences "proteins.fasta"
process_fasta "input.fasta" "headers.txt"



Functions use local variables to avoid name conflicts
Arguments accessed with $1, $2, etc. (just like scripts)
return sets an exit code (0–255), not a value
Use echo to return string/text values
Functions must be defined before they are called

title: Appendix C - GREP, Regular Expressions & Shell Scripting
text:
Error Handling

CodeBest Practices


#!/bin/bash

# Exit on error
set -e

# Exit on undefined variable
set -u

# Pipe failure detection
set -o pipefail

# Custom error handling
check_file() {
    if [ ! -f "$1" ]; then
        echo "Error: File $1 not found!" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
}

# Trap errors
trap 'echo "Error on line $LINENO"' ERR

# Try-catch style
if ! grep "pattern" file.txt > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "Pattern not found"
fi



Always include a shebang: #!/bin/bash
Comment your code: Explain what and why
Use meaningful variable names: GENE_COUNT not GC
Check inputs: Validate files exist and arguments are correct
Handle errors gracefully: Use set -e and error checking
Make scripts portable: Don’t hardcode paths
Use version control: Track changes with Git
Test incrementally: Build scripts step by step
Create help messages: Document usage
Log important steps: Keep records of what was done

title: Appendix C - GREP, Regular Expressions & Shell Scripting
text:
Complete Bioinformatics Script Example

CodeUsageInterpretation


#!/bin/bash
# Script: fasta_stats.sh
# Purpose: Calculate basic statistics for FASTA files

# Check arguments
if [ $# -ne 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 sequences.fasta"
    exit 1
fi

INPUT=$1

# Check if file exists
if [ ! -f "$INPUT" ]; then
    echo "Error: File $INPUT not found!"
    exit 1
fi

echo "=== FASTA Statistics for $INPUT ==="

# Count sequences
SEQ_COUNT=$(grep -c "^>" "$INPUT")
echo "Number of sequences: $SEQ_COUNT"

# Calculate total length
TOTAL_LENGTH=$(grep -v "^>" "$INPUT" | tr -d '\n' | wc -c)
echo "Total length: $TOTAL_LENGTH bp"

# Average length
AVG_LENGTH=$((TOTAL_LENGTH / SEQ_COUNT))
echo "Average length: $AVG_LENGTH bp"

# GC content
GC_COUNT=$(grep -v "^>" "$INPUT" | tr -d '\n' | grep -o "[GC]" | wc -l)
GC_PERCENT=$((GC_COUNT * 100 / TOTAL_LENGTH))
echo "GC content: $GC_PERCENT%"


# Make executable and run
chmod +x fasta_stats.sh
./fasta_stats.sh my_sequences.fasta


This script demonstrates several best practices:

Input validation (checking argument count and file existence)
Use of pipes for data processing
Meaningful variable names
Clear output formatting
Comments explaining each section

title: VS Code & Talapas
text:
What is VS Code? Visual Studio Code (VS Code) is a free, cross-platform editor from Microsoft that lets you browse file systems and edit text documents, including on remote servers such as Talapas. It has a built-in terminal that lets you access the command line, making it convenient for running scripts during the workshop.


Download and install VS Code from the official site:
https://code.visualstudio.com/download
Once installed, open VS Code. If a permissions pop-up appears, allow the requested access. From the main interface you can browse and open folders on your local computer.



VS Code can connect directly to the University of Oregon’s Talapas HPC cluster via SSH. Follow the steps below.


In the left sidebar, click the Extensions tab and search for ssh. Install the following extensions:

Remote — SSH
Remote Explorer
Remote — SSH: Editing Configuration Files (optional but recommended)




Open Remote Explorer from the left sidebar. Click the + icon next to SSH and enter the following command, replacing [yourduckid] with your UO Duck ID:
ssh [yourduckid]@login.talapas.uoregon.edu
Press Enter, then press Enter again to save to the default config file.



In the Remote Explorer dropdown, expand the SSH section. You should see login.talapas.uoregon.edu. Click the → arrow to start the connection, then:

Enter your Duck ID password when prompted (characters will not appear as you type).
When asked for Duo confirmation, type 1 and press Enter to receive a Duo push.
Once connected, a blue banner reading SSH: login.talapas.uoregon.edu will appear in the bottom-left corner.

Click Open Folder to browse directories on Talapas. It is helpful to open the base directory of your current project. You may also want a second window pointed at your home directory:
/home/[yourduckid]/nereus




Use the integrated terminal (Terminal → New Terminal) to run scripts directly on Talapas without leaving VS Code.
You can have multiple VS Code windows open — one for each server or project.
The file explorer on the left updates in real time, making it easy to navigate complex directory structures.

title: VS Code & Talapas
text:
What is Talapas?
Talapas is UO’s flagship high-performance computing (HPC) cluster, managed by Research Advanced Computing Services (RACS). The name comes from the Chinook word for coyote, the educator and keeper of knowledge. It combines centrally funded open-use resources with researcher-funded (“condo”) resources and, at last count, provides over 14,500 CPU cores, 129 TB of memory, and roughly 89 GPUs (AMD Milan, Intel Broadwell/Cascadelake/Icelake/Sapphire Rapids CPUs; NVIDIA A40 through H100 GPUs; memory configurations from 128 GB to 6 TB per node).

title: VS Code & Talapas
text:
Getting an Account
To work on Talapas you need a UO Duck ID and a Talapas account tied to a research group (called a PIRG — Principal Investigator Research Group). If you don’t yet have an account, request one at:
https://racs.uoregon.edu/request-access
Your Talapas username is your Duck ID (the part of your UO email before @uoregon.edu). Your password is the same University-wide credential and can be managed via UO’s password reset page.

title: VS Code & Talapas
text:
Logging In
The Talapas login host is login.talapas.uoregon.edu. Connect with SSH:
ssh [yourduckid]@login.talapas.uoregon.edu

For security, no characters are displayed while you type your password.
SSH logins use Duo two-factor authentication. When prompted, type 1 and press Enter to receive a Duo push, or follow the alternate options presented.
UO’s VPN is recommended for off-campus logins; with the VPN you can also reach direct login nodes (login1 … login4) if needed.
Mac and Linux users can set up an ED25519 SSH key to avoid typing a password every session (Duo will still prompt when required by policy).


Open OnDemand (browser-based access)
RACS also provides a web portal at https://ondemand.talapas.uoregon.edu/ (Chrome or Firefox recommended) that lets you edit files, launch a shell, start Jupyter, or open an interactive X11 desktop (via VNC) without any SSH client. It is the easiest way to run RStudio or Jupyter sessions on Talapas.

title: VS Code & Talapas
text:
File Systems and Storage
Every Talapas user has access to several directories on the ~3 PB IBM Elastic Storage System (GPFS):








Directory
Size
Purpose




/home/<duckid>/
250 GB
Personal files, scripts, dotfiles. Small software installs.


/projects/<PIRG>/
2 TB
Shared project data for your research group.


/scratch/<PIRG>/
20 TB
High-throughput working space. Files not accessed in the last 90 days are purged on a rolling basis.









Warning



You are responsible for backing up your data. RACS does not back up Talapas file systems. Some snapshotting exists for home and project directories, but it is not a substitute for your own backups.


File transfer options:

scp / sftp from the terminal.
Filezilla (cross-platform GUI for SCP/SFTP).
Globus — RACS’s recommended tool for large transfers, especially to/from the Large-Scale Research Storage (LSRS) system.

title: VS Code & Talapas
text:
Software Modules (Lmod)
Talapas uses Lmod to manage centrally installed software. Key commands:
module spider blast        # search for software by name
module avail               # list modules loadable without switching groups
module load samtools       # load a module (default version)
module load samtools/1.17  # load a specific version
module list                # show currently loaded modules
module unload samtools     # unload a module
module purge               # unload all modules
Modules are loaded per-shell — you will need to module load again in every new session (or in every SLURM job script).

title: VS Code & Talapas
text:
Partitions and the SLURM Scheduler
Talapas uses the SLURM job scheduler. Jobs are submitted to partitions (queues), each with different limits and hardware:








Partition
Typical use
Max wall time




compute
Standard CPU jobs
1 day


computelong
Long CPU jobs
14 days


gpu / gpulong
GPU jobs (short / long)
1 day / 14 d


memory / memorylong
High-memory CPU jobs (up to 4 TB RAM)
1 day / 14 d


interactive / interactivegpu
Short interactive sessions
12 h / 8 h


preempt
Access to all nodes; jobs may be preempted
Variable



All SLURM jobs must specify an account (your PIRG) for correct service charging. You can do this with --account=<myPIRG> on every command, or export it once in your ~/.bash_profile:
export SLURM_ACCOUNT=<myPIRG>
export SBATCH_ACCOUNT=<myPIRG>
See the separate SLURM tutorial for how to submit batch and interactive jobs. As a first rule: do not run heavy computations on the login nodes. Anything that takes more than a few seconds belongs in an interactive session (srun --pty bash) or a batch job (sbatch my_script.sh).

Talapas Service Desk — submit a ticket (include job IDs, scripts, and error messages).
RACS Office Hours — in-person help at the Knight Campus 1st Floor tables (check the Knowledge Base homepage for the current term’s schedule).

Slot

Contents

title: Tutorial 02 — scRNA-seq: Dimensionality Reduction & Clustering
text:
Learning goals

Run RunPCA() and read PCA diagnostic plots
Use ElbowPlot() to pick a defensible number of PCs
Run FindNeighbors() → FindClusters() at several resolutions and inspect the difference
Switch the active identity between clusterings with Idents()
Generate a UMAP and overlay cluster labels

title: Tutorial 02 — scRNA-seq: Dimensionality Reduction & Clustering
text:
Setup

library(Seurat)
library(tidyverse)
set.seed(2026)


nsclc.seurat.obj <- readRDS("../data/nsclc_preprocessed.rds")
nsclc.seurat.obj

title: Tutorial 02 — scRNA-seq: Dimensionality Reduction & Clustering
text:
Step 1 — Linear dimensionality reduction (PCA)

nsclc.seurat.obj <- RunPCA(
  nsclc.seurat.obj,
  features = VariableFeatures(object = nsclc.seurat.obj)
)

print(nsclc.seurat.obj[["pca"]], dims = 1:5, nfeatures = 5)
DimHeatmap(nsclc.seurat.obj, dims = 1, cells = 500, balanced = TRUE)







Think about it




PCA here runs on HVGs, not all genes. What would change if we included all genes?
DimHeatmap(dims = 1, cells = 500) — why show only 500 cells per PC?
What does a PC whose top loadings are all mitochondrial genes usually mean?



Show answers


PCs would be dominated by highly-expressed housekeeping genes whose variance simply reflects sequencing depth. HVG-restricted PCA isolates biologically-variable signal.
The heatmap orders cells along the PC and shows gene expression for the extreme low and high cells. 500 is enough to see the gradient; more would just smear the display and slow rendering.
It’s a technical PC tracking cell quality / stress rather than biology. If it dominates, consider regressing percent.mt in ScaleData, or excluding it from downstream clustering dims.

title: Tutorial 02 — scRNA-seq: Dimensionality Reduction & Clustering
section: Step 2 — Choose how many PCs to keep
text:
Step 2 — Choose how many PCs to keep

ElbowPlot(nsclc.seurat.obj, ndims = 40)







Think about it




Where is “the elbow” on an ElbowPlot, and why does it suggest a cutoff?
What’s a principled alternative to eyeballing the elbow?
Is it risky to choose too few PCs? Too many?



Show answers


The elbow is where the variance explained per PC flattens out — additional PCs stop adding much signal. Picking the elbow captures most of the biological structure without the noise-heavy tail.
JackStraw() + JackStrawPlot() (permutation-based significance) is more principled but slow on large datasets. Stuart et al. 2019 also showed that results are fairly robust across reasonable PC counts, so the heuristic is fine in practice.
Too few loses cell-type resolution (rare populations blur together). Too many pulls in noisy dimensions and inflates distances in the neighbor graph. Being slightly generous (e.g. 15–30) is usually safe.





n_pcs <- 15

title: Tutorial 02 — scRNA-seq: Dimensionality Reduction & Clustering
text:
Step 3 — Build the neighbor graph

nsclc.seurat.obj <- FindNeighbors(nsclc.seurat.obj, dims = 1:n_pcs)







Think about it




What graph does FindNeighbors() build?
Why operate on PC space rather than raw expression?



Show answers


It builds a k-nearest-neighbor graph (default k = 20) and then a shared-nearest-neighbor graph: two cells are connected if they share many nearest neighbors. The Jaccard-weighted SNN graph is what clustering runs on.
PC space is denoised, low-dimensional, and has well-behaved Euclidean distance. Raw expression is sparse, high-dimensional, and distance-distorted by the curse of dimensionality.

title: Tutorial 02 — scRNA-seq: Dimensionality Reduction & Clustering
text:
Step 4 — Cluster at several resolutions

nsclc.seurat.obj <- FindClusters(
  nsclc.seurat.obj,
  resolution = c(0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0)
)

head(nsclc.seurat.obj@meta.data)







Think about it




What does resolution control intuitively?
Each resolution creates its own RNA_snn_res.XYZ column in the metadata. How do you make one of them the active identity?
Try clustree::clustree(nsclc.seurat.obj, prefix = "RNA_snn_res."). What does it show?



Show answers


The granularity knob for the graph-based community detection. Higher resolution ⇒ smaller, more numerous clusters.
Idents(nsclc.seurat.obj) <- "RNA_snn_res.0.3" — or any of the resolution columns. DimPlot(..., group.by = "RNA_snn_res.0.3") also works without changing active idents.
A clustering tree: each row is a resolution, each node is a cluster, and edges show how cells flow from one resolution to the next. Stable clusters stay as one node across levels; unstable ones fragment. It’s a great way to pick a resolution visually.

title: Tutorial 02 — scRNA-seq: Dimensionality Reduction & Clustering
section: Step 5 — Visualize cluster counts across resolutions
text:
Step 5 — Visualize cluster counts across resolutions

# quick summary of cluster counts per resolution
nsclc.seurat.obj@meta.data |>
  select(starts_with("RNA_snn_res.")) |>
  map_int(~ length(unique(.x)))







Think about it




At res = 0.1 you might see 6 clusters; at res = 1.0 you might see 20+. Which is “right”?
Does changing resolution change the underlying biology or just how the same biology is partitioned?



Show answers


Neither is objectively right. Resolution is a knob — choose the one that matches the biological question. For a broad cell-type atlas, lower resolution is fine; to split subtypes, you need higher. Confirm with markers.
Just the partitioning. The underlying graph and cell embeddings are unchanged — resolution decides where to draw the cluster boundaries.

title: Tutorial 02 — scRNA-seq: Dimensionality Reduction & Clustering
section: Step 6 — Pick a resolution, set identities, and plot
text:
Step 6 — Pick a resolution, set identities, and plot

Idents(nsclc.seurat.obj) <- "RNA_snn_res.0.5"
DimPlot(
  nsclc.seurat.obj,
  group.by = "RNA_snn_res.0.5",
  label    = TRUE
)

title: Tutorial 02 — scRNA-seq: Dimensionality Reduction & Clustering
text:
Step 7 — Non-linear embedding (UMAP)

nsclc.seurat.obj <- RunUMAP(nsclc.seurat.obj, dims = 1:n_pcs)
DimPlot(nsclc.seurat.obj, reduction = "umap", label = TRUE)







Think about it




Clusters are defined on the graph, not on UMAP. How can clustering and UMAP disagree visually?
Why shouldn’t you compare UMAP distances quantitatively between clusters?



Show answers


UMAP is a separate non-linear projection. A cluster may occasionally split visually on UMAP while still being one graph community — and vice versa. Trust the graph-based cluster call; use UMAP as a sanity check.
UMAP preserves local neighborhoods but distorts global distances to fit in 2D. A cluster that looks far away on UMAP isn’t necessarily biologically more distant than one nearby.

title: Tutorial 02 — scRNA-seq: Dimensionality Reduction & Clustering
text:
Save and continue to Tutorial 03

saveRDS(nsclc.seurat.obj, file = "../data/nsclc_clustered.rds")







Tip



Continue with Tutorial 03 — Markers & Cell Type Annotation.

Label
Meaning

title: Tutorial 03 — scRNA-seq: Markers & Cell Type Annotation
text:
Learning goals

Use FindMarkers / FindAllMarkers appropriately
Read each of the five standard marker-visualization plots and know when to pick which
Assign cell-type labels manually and with a reference-based classifier
Cross-check manual and automatic labels to catch ambiguity

title: Tutorial 03 — scRNA-seq: Markers & Cell Type Annotation
text:
Setup

library(Seurat)
library(tidyverse)

# For automatic annotation
# BiocManager::install(c("SingleR", "celldex"))
# library(SingleR)
# library(celldex)

set.seed(2026)


nsclc.seurat.obj <- readRDS("../data/nsclc_clustered.rds")
Idents(nsclc.seurat.obj) <- "RNA_snn_res.0.5"
nsclc.seurat.obj

title: Tutorial 03 — scRNA-seq: Markers & Cell Type Annotation
section: Step 1 — Find markers for one cluster
text:
Step 1 — Find markers for one cluster

cluster1.markers <- FindMarkers(nsclc.seurat.obj, ident.1 = 1, min.pct = 0.25)
head(cluster1.markers, 10)







Think about it




What does min.pct = 0.25 do, and why is that a useful default?
The output has avg_log2FC, pct.1, pct.2. Which gene would you pick as a specific marker for this cluster?



Show answers


Only tests genes detected in ≥ 25% of cells in at least one of the two groups. Prunes low-expression noise and speeds the test up significantly.
A specific marker has high pct.1 and low pct.2 (expressed in almost all cluster-1 cells, almost none elsewhere) and high positive avg_log2FC. Markers that are broadly expressed (high pct.2) may be real but aren’t specific.

title: Tutorial 03 — scRNA-seq: Markers & Cell Type Annotation
section: Step 2 — Markers distinguishing one cluster from a pair
text:
Step 2 — Markers distinguishing one cluster from a pair

cluster5.markers <- FindMarkers(
  nsclc.seurat.obj,
  ident.1 = 5,
  ident.2 = c(0, 3),
  min.pct = 0.25
)
head(cluster5.markers, 10)







Think about it




Why might you compare cluster 5 only to clusters 0 and 3, rather than to all other cells?
What’s a situation where you’d want ident.1 vs ident.2 to be two groups of clusters (e.g. ident.1 = c(5, 7))?



Show answers


If 0 and 3 look similar on UMAP or markers to cluster 5, you want genes that specifically separate 5 from those neighbors — genome-wide vs-all tests can be dominated by genes separating cluster 5 from totally unrelated populations.
When you have biological groups of clusters, e.g. merging two T-cell subtypes (5 + 7) to compare against NK cells (3 + 8). Grouping lets you test at the lineage level rather than the cluster level.

title: Tutorial 03 — scRNA-seq: Markers & Cell Type Annotation
text:
Step 3 — Markers for every cluster

nsclc.markers <- FindAllMarkers(
  nsclc.seurat.obj,
  only.pos        = TRUE,
  min.pct         = 0.25,
  logfc.threshold = 0.25
)

top_markers <- nsclc.markers |>
  group_by(cluster) |>
  slice_max(avg_log2FC, n = 5)
top_markers







Think about it




only.pos = TRUE — what do you lose?
For condition-level DE (treated vs control), is FindAllMarkers the right tool?



Show answers


You only see genes up in the cluster of interest. Sometimes a gene is a strong negative marker (consistently lower than elsewhere) and also diagnostic. Rerun without only.pos if you want both directions.
No. FindAllMarkers / FindMarkers treat each cell as an independent observation, which is anti-conservative for condition-level comparisons across biological samples. For that, use pseudobulk + DESeq2/edgeR/limma (see the Pseudobulk extra lecture).

title: Tutorial 03 — scRNA-seq: Markers & Cell Type Annotation
text:
Step 4 — Visualizing markers

4a. Violin plots — expression distribution per cluster

top_genes <- top_markers |> ungroup() |> slice_head(n = 6) |> pull(gene)
VlnPlot(nsclc.seurat.obj, features = top_genes, pt.size = 0, ncol = 3)



4b. Feature plots — expression on UMAP

FeaturePlot(nsclc.seurat.obj, features = top_genes[1:4])



4c. Heatmap — top markers across clusters

top10 <- nsclc.markers |> group_by(cluster) |> slice_max(avg_log2FC, n = 10)
DoHeatmap(nsclc.seurat.obj, features = top10$gene) + NoLegend()



4d. Ridge plots — expression density per cluster

RidgePlot(nsclc.seurat.obj, features = top_genes[1:4], ncol = 2)



4e. Dot plot — % expressing × mean expression

DotPlot(nsclc.seurat.obj, features = top_genes) + RotatedAxis()







Think about it




Which of the five plot types is best for showing a 20-gene marker panel across 10 clusters on one page?
Which is best for checking whether a single gene has spatial structure on the UMAP?
Which is most misleading if your cluster sizes are very unequal, and why?



Show answers


Dot plot — compact, shows both percent-expressing (dot size) and mean expression (color) in a genes × clusters grid.
Feature plot — overlays expression on the 2-D embedding so you can see geography.
Heatmap can be misleading: it shows cells, not clusters, so a dominant cluster (say 40% of cells) visually monopolizes the plot. Dot plot normalizes per cluster and avoids this.

title: Tutorial 03 — scRNA-seq: Markers & Cell Type Annotation
text:
Step 5 — Manual annotation

# Example only — replace these with the labels appropriate for YOUR markers.
# Work through the top markers per cluster, look them up in CellMarker 2.0
# or PanglaoDB, and decide the biology.

# new.cluster.ids <- c("T cells", "Macrophages", "Epithelial",
#                      "Fibroblasts", "B cells", ...)
# names(new.cluster.ids) <- levels(nsclc.seurat.obj)
# nsclc.seurat.obj <- RenameIdents(nsclc.seurat.obj, new.cluster.ids)
# nsclc.seurat.obj$celltype_manual <- Idents(nsclc.seurat.obj)

# DimPlot(nsclc.seurat.obj, reduction = "umap", label = TRUE, repel = TRUE) +
#   NoLegend()







Think about it




Two clusters share KRT8/KRT18 as top markers. Are they the same cell type?
What’s the risk of assigning a label from a single marker gene?



Show answers


Not necessarily — KRT8/18 are broad epithelial markers. Both clusters are likely epithelial, but could be distinct subtypes (tumor vs. normal epithelium, alveolar type I vs. II, etc.). You need discriminating markers on top of the shared ones.
Many markers are non-specific, and biological “marker genes” are often graded rather than binary. Label from combinations (a marker plus absence of another marker) and always cross-check with an automatic method.

title: Tutorial 03 — scRNA-seq: Markers & Cell Type Annotation
text:
Step 6 — Automatic annotation with SingleR

# library(SingleR)
# library(celldex)
#
# # Pick a reference appropriate for your tissue
# ref <- celldex::HumanPrimaryCellAtlasData()
#
# # SingleR expects log-normalized expression
# expr <- GetAssayData(nsclc.seurat.obj, slot = "data")
#
# singler.labels <- SingleR(
#   test    = expr,
#   ref     = ref,
#   labels  = ref$label.main
# )
#
# nsclc.seurat.obj$celltype_singler <- singler.labels$labels
#
# DimPlot(nsclc.seurat.obj, group.by = "celltype_singler", label = TRUE, repel = TRUE) +
#   NoLegend()







Think about it




How would you check whether SingleR is confident about a label vs just picking the best of a bad set?
What would you do when SingleR and your manual annotation disagree on a cluster?



Show answers


Inspect singler.labels$scores and the pruned.labels column (labels it declines to assign when score gaps are small). You can also plot plotScoreHeatmap(singler.labels) to see per-cell confidence.
Disagreements often flag real ambiguity — rare subtypes, transitional states, or references that don’t include your biology (e.g. a tumor reference vs a healthy-tissue reference). Re-check markers, pick a more tissue-appropriate reference, and document the uncertainty.

title: Tutorial 03 — scRNA-seq: Markers & Cell Type Annotation
text:
Step 7 — Save the final object

saveRDS(nsclc.seurat.obj, file = "../data/nsclc_annotated.rds")

title: Tutorial — SLURM Basics on Talapas
text:
This tutorial covers the basics of using SLURM (the job scheduler on the University of Oregon’s Talapas HPC cluster) — both in interactive mode and through batch scripts. It is aimed at workshop participants who have a Duck ID and Talapas account and are ready to run their first jobs.
All examples are specific to UO and Talapas. If you have not yet logged in to Talapas for the first time, see the VS Code & Talapas appendix first.
Material is adapted from the Talapas Quick Start Guide and the How-To articles in the Talapas Knowledge Base.






How to use this page



Read this page top to bottom the first time. Later, treat it as a reference — the commands here cover ~95% of what you will do in the workshop.

title: Tutorial — SLURM Basics on Talapas
text:
This tutorial covers the basics of using SLURM (the job scheduler on the University of Oregon’s Talapas HPC cluster) — both in interactive mode and through batch scripts. It is aimed at workshop participants who have a Duck ID and Talapas account and are ready to run their first jobs.
All examples are specific to UO and Talapas. If you have not yet logged in to Talapas for the first time, see the VS Code & Talapas appendix first.
Material is adapted from the Talapas Quick Start Guide and the How-To articles in the Talapas Knowledge Base.






How to use this page



Read this page top to bottom the first time. Later, treat it as a reference — the commands here cover ~95% of what you will do in the workshop.

title: Tutorial — SLURM Basics on Talapas
text:
1. Why SLURM?
Talapas is a shared cluster with thousands of CPU cores, hundreds of GPUs, and many terabytes of RAM. Dozens of users compete for those resources at any given time. SLURM (Simple Linux Utility for Resource Management) is the scheduler that decides when, where, and with what resources each job runs.
At UO, this means:

You never run heavy computation directly on a login node (login.talapas.uoregon.edu). Login nodes are for editing, short commands, and submitting jobs only.
Instead, you ask SLURM for resources — CPUs, GPUs, memory, time — and SLURM allocates a compute node for you.
Every job at UO must specify an account (your PIRG, Principal Investigator Research Group) for correct service charging.

title: Tutorial — SLURM Basics on Talapas
text:
2. Prerequisites
Before you start:

A Talapas account tied to a PIRG (request one at racs.uoregon.edu/request-access).
You have SSH’d into Talapas at least once:
ssh <yourduckid>@login.talapas.uoregon.edu
You know the name of your PIRG (group). If unsure, run groups after logging in — your PIRG(s) will be listed alongside system groups (which you cannot charge to).

Optionally, set your account once in ~/.bash_profile so you don’t type --account= every time:
echo 'export SLURM_ACCOUNT=<myPIRG>'  >> ~/.bash_profile
echo 'export SBATCH_ACCOUNT=<myPIRG>' >> ~/.bash_profile
source ~/.bash_profile
Replace <myPIRG> with your actual PIRG name.

title: Tutorial — SLURM Basics on Talapas
section: 3. Key SLURM commands at a glance
text:
3. Key SLURM commands at a glance







Command
Purpose




sinfo
Show partitions and node states.


squeue -u $USER
Show your queued/running jobs.


sbatch script.sh
Submit a batch job.


srun ... --pty bash
Start an interactive session on a compute node.


scancel <JOBID>
Cancel a queued or running job.


sacct -j <JOBID>
Show accounting info (state, memory, runtime) for past jobs.


seff <JOBID>
Friendly summary of resources used by a finished job.









Key SLURM flags you will use constantly




--account=<myPIRG> — PIRG / account for charging (required).
--partition=<name> — queue (e.g. compute, gpu, interactive).
--time=<HH:MM:SS> or --time=<minutes> — wall-clock time limit.
--mem=<size> — total memory per node (e.g. 8G, 100M).
--mem-per-cpu=<size> — memory per core.
--cpus-per-task=<N> — cores for a multi-threaded task.
--gpus=<N> — request GPUs (paired with --partition=gpu).
--constraint=gpu-10gb / gpu-40gb / gpu-80gb — GPU memory tier.

title: Tutorial — SLURM Basics on Talapas
text:
4. Talapas partitions (queues)
Every job must target a partition. Common ones at UO:








Partition
What it’s for
Max time




compute
Standard CPU jobs.
1 day


computelong
Long-running CPU jobs.
14 days


gpu / gpulong
GPU jobs, short / long.
1 / 14 d


memory / memorylong
High-memory CPU jobs (up to 4 TB RAM).
1 / 14 d


interactive / interactivegpu
Short interactive sessions.
12 / 8 h


preempt
All nodes; your job may be preempted by its owner.
Variable



Run sinfo to see the current state of each partition on the cluster.

title: Tutorial — SLURM Basics on Talapas
text:
5. Interactive mode — srun
Use an interactive session when you want a shell on a compute node — for testing scripts, exploring data, or running applications like RStudio or Python interactively. This is the mode you will use most often during the workshop.

5.1 A minimum interactive session
From a Talapas login node, run:
srun --account=<myPIRG> \
     --partition=compute \
     --time=1:00:00 \
     --mem=4G \
     --cpus-per-task=2 \
     --pty bash
What each flag does:

--account — your PIRG (required on Talapas; omit if you set SLURM_ACCOUNT in your profile).
--partition=compute — standard CPU queue.
--time=1:00:00 — 1 hour; after this the session is killed.
--mem=4G — 4 GB of RAM total on that node.
--cpus-per-task=2 — two CPU cores.
--pty bash — give me a pseudo-terminal running bash.

When the resources are available, your prompt will change to show the hostname of a compute node (e.g. n0123). You are now running on a compute node. When you’re done, type exit to release the resources — do not leave idle sessions running.


5.2 Using the short interactive partition
For short, quick sessions the interactive partition is usually faster to start:
srun --account=<myPIRG> --partition=interactive --time=2:00:00 --mem=8G --pty bash


5.3 Interactive GPU session
srun --account=<myPIRG> \
     --partition=interactivegpu \
     --time=1:00:00 \
     --nodes=1 --ntasks=1 --cpus-per-task=2 \
     --mem=16G \
     --gpus=1 --constraint=gpu-10gb \
     --pty bash
--constraint=gpu-10gb (or gpu-40gb / gpu-80gb) pins the job to a GPU of that memory tier.


5.4 Graphical interactive jobs
If you need a GUI (e.g. RStudio, MATLAB), two options:

X11 forwarding — SSH in with ssh -Y and then:
srun --account=<myPIRG> --x11 --partition=compute \
     --time=2:00:00 --mem=4G --pty bash
You need an X server running on your laptop (XQuartz on macOS, VcXsrv on Windows).
Open OnDemand (recommended and simpler) — open https://ondemand.talapas.uoregon.edu/ in Chrome or Firefox, sign in with your Duck ID, and launch an RStudio, Jupyter, or full desktop session from the web portal. No X server needed.







Don’t hoard nodes



Interactive jobs reserve their resources even when you are not actively typing. As soon as you are done, exit the shell so other users can use the node.

title: Tutorial — SLURM Basics on Talapas
text:
6. Batch mode — sbatch
For longer-running analyses (which is most real workshop work), write a batch script and submit it with sbatch. The script runs without an interactive terminal: you can log out and the job keeps running.

6.1 A first batch script
Save the following as hostname.sbatch:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --account=<myPIRG>        ### PIRG / account for charging
#SBATCH --partition=compute       ### queue
#SBATCH --job-name=hello          ### visible in squeue
#SBATCH --output=hello_%j.out     ### %j = job ID
#SBATCH --error=hello_%j.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00           ### HH:MM:SS
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=1
#SBATCH --mem=100M

echo "Hello from $(hostname) at $(date)"
Submit it:
sbatch hostname.sbatch
SLURM prints a job ID:
Submitted batch job 123456
Check its status:
squeue -j 123456
# or
squeue -u $USER
State codes you’ll see: PD (pending), R (running), CG (completing). Once it finishes, two files appear in the directory you submitted from: hello_123456.out (stdout) and hello_123456.err (stderr).


6.2 Anatomy of #SBATCH directives
All #SBATCH lines must be at the top of the script, before any other commands. A few additional flags worth knowing:
#SBATCH --mem-per-cpu=4G          ### per-core memory (alternative to --mem)
#SBATCH --mail-type=END,FAIL      ### email on end/failure
#SBATCH --mail-user=you@uoregon.edu
#SBATCH --array=1-10              ### job array: 10 independent tasks


6.3 A realistic scRNA-seq job
A batch script that loads R and runs a Seurat QC pipeline looks like this:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --account=<myPIRG>
#SBATCH --partition=compute
#SBATCH --job-name=seurat_qc
#SBATCH --output=logs/seurat_qc_%j.out
#SBATCH --error=logs/seurat_qc_%j.err
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=64G

set -euo pipefail

# Load environment
module purge
module load R/4.3.2

# Run the pipeline
cd /projects/<myPIRG>/<yourduckid>/scrnaseq
Rscript scripts/01_qc_preprocess.R
Note how module purge and module load happen inside the script — modules don’t survive from the login node into the job.


6.4 GPU batch job
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --account=<myPIRG>
#SBATCH --partition=gpu
#SBATCH --job-name=scvi
#SBATCH --output=logs/scvi_%j.out
#SBATCH --error=logs/scvi_%j.err
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=64G
#SBATCH --gpus=1
#SBATCH --constraint=gpu-40gb

module purge
module load python/3.11
source ~/envs/scvi/bin/activate

python scripts/train_scvi.py

title: Tutorial — SLURM Basics on Talapas
text:
7. Choosing the right resources

Smaller requests run sooner. SLURM finds small gaps in the schedule more easily, so over-requesting memory or time will delay your job.
But specify enough. If your job exceeds its --mem or --time, SLURM kills it (OUT_OF_MEMORY or TIMEOUT). Err slightly on the high side the first time you run a new pipeline.
The default per-core memory is about 3.7 GB. Request --mem-per-cpu or --mem explicitly if you need more.
The compute partition default wall time is 24 hours; computelong defaults to two weeks. Both cap at those values.

After a job finishes, check what it actually used:
seff 123456
or:
sacct -j 123456 --format=JobID,JobName,ReqMem,MaxRSS,Elapsed,State,ExitCode
Use MaxRSS (peak memory) and Elapsed (wall time) to right-size future submissions.

title: Tutorial — SLURM Basics on Talapas
text:
8. Monitoring, cancelling, and troubleshooting
# See your jobs
squeue -u $USER

# Watch a specific job
squeue -j 123456

# Cancel a job
scancel 123456

# Cancel all your pending jobs
scancel -u $USER -t PENDING

# Summary of a finished job
seff 123456

# Detailed accounting (multiple steps)
sacct -j 123456 --format=JobID,State,ExitCode,Elapsed,MaxRSS,ReqMem
Common reasons a job stays PD (pending):

Priority / Resources — normal queueing; too many users ahead of you, or the resources you asked for are not free yet.
QOSMaxJobsPerUserLimit — you have hit a per-user submission limit; let earlier jobs finish.
ReqNodeNotAvail, Reserved for maintenance — your --time would run into an upcoming quarterly maintenance window. Shorten --time, or add --time-min= to let SLURM start the job sooner with a reduced limit:
#SBATCH --time=0-08:00:00
#SBATCH --time-min=0-01:00:00

See also the Knowledge Base FAQ Why is my SLURM job not running?.

title: Tutorial — SLURM Basics on Talapas
section: 9. Being a good cluster citizen at UO
text:
9. Being a good cluster citizen at UO
Talapas is shared across all of UO’s research community. Please:

Never run intensive work on login nodes; use srun or sbatch.
Don’t leave idle interactive sessions open. exit when you’re done.
Don’t request more CPUs, memory, or GPUs than you will actually use.
Put large output (caches, intermediate files) in /scratch/<PIRG>/, not in /home/.

Remember that /scratch purges files not accessed in 90 days.

Back up anything you care about. RACS does not back up Talapas.
If you have a deadline and need special scheduling, email RACS rather than trying to game the queue.

title: Tutorial — SLURM Basics on Talapas
text:
10. Where to go next

Talapas Partition List — up-to-date details on hardware per partition.
SLURM Workload Manager documentation — the canonical SLURM reference.

RACS Office Hours — Knight Campus 1st Floor tables; see the Talapas Knowledge Base homepage for the current term’s schedule.

title: Exercise — scATAC-seq with Signac
text:
A parallel to the scRNA-seq tutorial series — but for chromatin accessibility (scATAC-seq). The workflow follows the standard Signac pipeline from the Stuart Lab, applied to the 10x PBMC 10k scATAC-seq dataset.

Reference vignette: Signac PBMC scATAC-seq vignette
Workflow source: Khushbu Patel, single-cell ATAC-seq standard workflow







How to use this page



The rendered HTML shows the code but does not execute it. To run it:

Download the .qmd source and open it in RStudio.
Download the three data files listed below into ../data/.
Work through the chunks, flipping eval: true as you go.









What’s different about ATAC-seq?



ATAC-seq measures open chromatin (regions accessible to Tn5), not transcription. The raw unit is a fragment (a pair of Tn5 cut sites). The “features” are peaks — genomic intervals of accessibility — rather than genes. Most of the Seurat idioms carry over, but the normalization, dimensionality reduction, and QC metrics are ATAC-specific.

title: Exercise — scATAC-seq with Signac
text:
Dataset — PBMC 10k scATAC-seq (10x Genomics)
What it is. Approximately ten-thousand human peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) from a single healthy donor, run on the 10x Chromium Single Cell ATAC v1 assay. This is the canonical tutorial dataset used across Signac, ArchR, and EpiScanpy vignettes.

Landing page: 10x Genomics — PBMC 10k scATAC-seq
Reference genome: hg19 / GRCh37 (hence EnsDb.Hsapiens.v75 below)
Chemistry: 10x Chromium Single Cell ATAC v1
Cell count: ~8,700 post-filter cells







Warning



Only the v1 release is pinned to hg19. The Next GEM v1.1 re-release of the same sample is mapped to hg38 and uses file names prefixed with atac_v1_pbmc_10k_nextgem_*. This exercise assumes the original hg19 release.

title: Exercise — scATAC-seq with Signac
text:
Learning goals
By the end of this exercise you will be able to:

Load peak-count and fragment data into a Signac ChromatinAssay inside a Seurat object
Compute and interpret ATAC-specific QC metrics (nucleosome signal, TSS enrichment, blacklist ratio, peak-read fraction)
Run TF-IDF normalization and SVD (LSI) on accessibility data
Produce a UMAP and cluster scATAC-seq cells

title: Exercise — scATAC-seq with Signac
text:
Setup

# One-time install:
# remotes::install_github("stuart-lab/signac", ref = "develop")
# install.packages("Matrix", type = "source")
# install.packages("irlba",  type = "source")
# BiocManager::install("EnsDb.Hsapiens.v75")

library(Signac)
library(Seurat)
library(EnsDb.Hsapiens.v75)
library(tidyverse)

set.seed(2026)

title: Exercise — scATAC-seq with Signac
text:
Data files and data dictionary
You will need three files from the 10x PBMC 10k scATAC-seq release, placed under ../data/. All three are available under “Output and supplemental files” on the 10x landing page.








File
Size
Purpose




atac_v1_pbmc_10k_filtered_peak_bc_matrix.h5
~30 MB
peak × barcode counts (sparse, filtered to called cells)


atac_v1_pbmc_10k_fragments.tsv.gz + .tbi
~1.8 GB + ~1 MB
per-fragment record (tabix-indexed)


atac_v1_pbmc_10k_singlecell.csv
~20 MB
per-barcode Cell Ranger ATAC summary




filtered_peak_bc_matrix.h5








Dimension
Type
Description




rows
character
Peak coordinates as chrom:start-end strings (e.g. chr1:10279-10779). Peaks are fixed-width genomic intervals called by Cell Ranger.


cols
character
Cell barcodes (16 bp + -1 GEM-well suffix).


values
integer
Number of Tn5 fragments from that cell whose midpoint falls inside that peak.





fragments.tsv.gz (bgzipped, tabix-indexed)
Tab-separated, one row per unique fragment. Five columns:








#
Column
Description




1
chrom
Chromosome (chr1 … chrY, plus contigs).


2
start
0-based start of the fragment (= position of one Tn5 cut).


3
end
0-based exclusive end of the fragment (= position of the other Tn5 cut).


4
cell_barcode
10x cell barcode (matches column names of the peak matrix).


5
duplicate_count
Number of PCR-duplicate reads collapsed into this fragment (> 1 means the same fragment was sequenced multiple times).





singlecell.csv (per-barcode Cell Ranger summary)
One row per barcode. Relevant columns (not exhaustive — 10x produces ~20 columns):







Column
Description




barcode
10x barcode string (row names in read.csv(..., row.names = 1)).


is__cell_barcode
Boolean flag, Cell Ranger’s call of “this barcode is a real cell”.


total
Total read pairs assigned to this barcode.


passed_filters
Reads passing all Cell Ranger ATAC QC filters.


peak_region_fragments
Fragments falling within a called peak. Numerator for pct_reads_in_peaks.


blacklist_region_fragments
Fragments falling inside the ENCODE blacklist. Numerator for blacklist_ratio.


TSS_fragments
Fragments within ± flank of a TSS.


mitochondrial
Fragments mapping to chrM.


duplicate
PCR-duplicate fragments.



These columns feed directly into the ATAC QC metrics computed in Step 4.

title: Exercise — scATAC-seq with Signac
section: Step 1 — Peek at the fragment file
text:
Step 1 — Peek at the fragment file

frag.file <- read.delim(
  "../data/atac_v1_pbmc_10k_fragments.tsv.gz",
  header = FALSE,
  nrows  = 10
)
head(frag.file)







Think about it




A fragment file has one line per Tn5 fragment. What are the five columns typically recorded?
Why do we keep the fragment file around instead of just using the peak-counts matrix?



Show answers


chrom, start, end, cell_barcode, duplicate_count (number of reads collapsed to that fragment).
Many ATAC analyses (TSS enrichment, nucleosome signal, coverage tracks, motif activity) work at the fragment level, not the peak level. The fragment file is the raw, peak-independent record; peaks can be recomputed later.

title: Exercise — scATAC-seq with Signac
section: Step 2 — Load the peaks matrix and create the object
text:
Step 2 — Load the peaks matrix and create the object

counts <- Read10X_h5("../data/atac_v1_pbmc_10k_filtered_peak_bc_matrix.h5")
counts[1:5, 1:5]

chrom_assay <- CreateChromatinAssay(
  counts       = counts,
  sep          = c(":", "-"),
  fragments    = "../data/atac_v1_pbmc_10k_fragments.tsv.gz",
  min.cells    = 10,
  min.features = 200
)

metadata <- read.csv(
  file      = "../data/atac_v1_pbmc_10k_singlecell.csv",
  header    = TRUE,
  row.names = 1
)

pbmc <- CreateSeuratObject(
  counts    = chrom_assay,
  meta.data = metadata,
  assay     = "ATAC"
)

pbmc







Think about it




The row names of an RNA assay are gene symbols. What do row names of a ChromatinAssay look like, and why?
We pass both counts and fragments when building the ChromatinAssay. Why is that redundant-looking pairing actually useful?



Show answers


Peak coordinates — strings like chr1:100234-100734. Peaks are genomic intervals, not annotated features, so the natural identifier is the interval itself.
counts is the fast aggregated view (peak × cell). fragments retains per-fragment, per-base precision for downstream peak recomputation, coverage plots, TSS enrichment, motif accessibility, etc. You want both.

title: Exercise — scATAC-seq with Signac
text:
Step 3 — Add gene annotation

annotations <- GetGRangesFromEnsDb(ensdb = EnsDb.Hsapiens.v75)
seqlevels(annotations) <- paste0("chr", seqlevels(annotations))  # UCSC naming
Annotation(pbmc) <- annotations







Think about it




The data is mapped to hg19 (GRCh37). Why EnsDb.Hsapiens.v75 specifically?
What would you change for a dataset mapped to hg38 or to mm10?



Show answers


Ensembl v75 is the last annotation release pinned to GRCh37 / hg19. Using a matching annotation is essential — otherwise TSSs and gene bodies will be in the wrong places.
hg38: use a current human EnsDb (e.g. EnsDb.Hsapiens.v86 or newer). mm10: use EnsDb.Mmusculus.v79 or equivalent. Chromosome-name style also differs (1 vs chr1); convert to match the counts.

title: Exercise — scATAC-seq with Signac
text:
Step 4 — Compute ATAC-specific QC metrics

pbmc <- NucleosomeSignal(pbmc)
pbmc <- TSSEnrichment(object = pbmc, fast = FALSE)

pbmc$blacklist_ratio    <- pbmc$blacklist_region_fragments / pbmc$peak_region_fragments
pbmc$pct_reads_in_peaks <- pbmc$peak_region_fragments / pbmc$passed_filters * 100

colnames(pbmc@meta.data)







Think about it




What does a high nucleosome signal indicate?
Why is TSS enrichment a quality metric, not a biology metric?
What is the ENCODE blacklist, and why exclude it?



Show answers


High nucleosome signal means an excess of multi-nucleosome-length fragments (vs sub-nucleosomal). Good ATAC libraries show a clear bias toward sub-nucleosomal fragments in open chromatin. High signal flags over-digested or low-quality cells.
Because active TSSs are reliably open in essentially all cells. Good cells show a sharp accessibility peak at TSSs; low enrichment at TSSs signals poor Tn5 activity or barcode contamination regardless of cell type.
A curated list of regions with anomalously high signal in any experiment (repeats, centromeres, mappability artefacts). Reads falling there are technical noise; a high blacklist ratio indicates a problematic cell or library.

title: Exercise — scATAC-seq with Signac
text:
Step 5 — Visualize QC

a1 <- DensityScatter(pbmc, x = "nCount_ATAC",
                     y = "TSS.enrichment", log_x = TRUE, quantiles = TRUE)
a2 <- DensityScatter(pbmc, x = "nucleosome_signal",
                     y = "TSS.enrichment", log_x = TRUE, quantiles = TRUE)
a1 | a2

VlnPlot(
  object   = pbmc,
  features = c("nCount_ATAC", "nFeature_ATAC", "TSS.enrichment",
               "nucleosome_signal", "blacklist_ratio", "pct_reads_in_peaks"),
  pt.size  = 0.1,
  ncol     = 6
)







Think about it




In the nCount_ATAC × TSS.enrichment density plot, where should good cells sit?
What would a blob at high nCount_ATAC and low TSS enrichment suggest?



Show answers


Middle-to-high nCount_ATAC with high TSS enrichment — enough reads, concentrated at bona-fide open regions.
Many reads but they’re not at TSSs. Classic signature of a bad library, ambient reads, or reads from dead cells — not useful signal.

title: Exercise — scATAC-seq with Signac
text:
Step 6 — Filter low-quality cells

pbmc <- subset(
  x = pbmc,
  subset = nCount_ATAC       > 3000  &
           nCount_ATAC       < 30000 &
           pct_reads_in_peaks > 15   &
           blacklist_ratio   < 0.05  &
           nucleosome_signal < 4     &
           TSS.enrichment    > 3
)
pbmc







Think about it




These thresholds come from the Signac PBMC vignette. Would you use them as-is on another dataset?
What’s the analogue of percent.mt in ATAC, and is it relevant?



Show answers


No — ATAC metric distributions shift with tissue, depth, and protocol version. Use the vignette thresholds as a starting point and always inspect the violins and density plots first.
Direct analogue doesn’t exist (ATAC doesn’t measure RNA). The quality proxies are TSS enrichment, nucleosome signal, blacklist ratio, and pct_reads_in_peaks — each captures a different failure mode.

title: Exercise — scATAC-seq with Signac
section: Step 7 — TF-IDF normalization, top features, SVD
text:
Step 7 — TF-IDF normalization, top features, SVD

pbmc <- RunTFIDF(pbmc)
pbmc <- FindTopFeatures(pbmc, min.cutoff = "q0")
pbmc <- RunSVD(pbmc)

DepthCor(pbmc)







Think about it




RNA uses LogNormalize. ATAC uses TF-IDF. Why the different normalization?
Why is the first LSI component often discarded (see DepthCor)?



Show answers


ATAC data is binary-ish — most peaks are either accessible (1) or not (0) per cell. TF-IDF (term-frequency × inverse document-frequency) weighs peaks by rarity, which is well-suited to sparse binary data. LogNormalize is tuned for count RNA data.
The first SVD component typically tracks sequencing depth / library size rather than biology. DepthCor shows correlation of each LSI component with depth; if component 1 is highly correlated, skip it and use dims 2:30 downstream.

title: Exercise — scATAC-seq with Signac
text:
Step 8 — Non-linear embedding and clustering

pbmc <- RunUMAP(pbmc, reduction = "lsi", dims = 2:30)
pbmc <- FindNeighbors(pbmc, reduction = "lsi", dims = 2:30)
pbmc <- FindClusters(pbmc, algorithm = 3)   # SLM

DimPlot(pbmc, label = TRUE) + NoLegend()







Think about it




Why dims = 2:30 instead of 1:30?
algorithm = 3 selects the SLM (Smart Local Moving) algorithm. Name one case when you’d use it instead of the default Louvain.



Show answers


Because LSI component 1 is the depth-confounded one we saw in DepthCor. Starting at 2 keeps only biologically-meaningful dimensions.
SLM often produces more stable partitions than basic Louvain on large, noisy graphs (e.g. scATAC) because it allows within-cluster re-optimization. Leiden (algorithm 4) is usually the modern default; SLM remains a reasonable alternative.

title: Exercise — scATAC-seq with Signac
text:
Save the object

saveRDS(pbmc, file = "../data/pbmc_atac_clustered.rds")

title: Exercise — scATAC-seq with Signac
text:
Stretch goals

Gene activity — convert peaks to per-gene accessibility scores with GeneActivity(), then run a Seurat-style RNA-like analysis on top
Motif enrichment — use chromVAR or Signac’s RunChromVAR for TF-motif activity per cell
Multi-modal integration — integrate scRNA-seq + scATAC-seq from the same cells (multiome) using FindTransferAnchors
