title: Tutorial 01 — scRNA-seq: QC & Preprocessing
text:
The first of a three-part series walking the standard Seurat scRNA-seq workflow, using the public 10x Genomics NSCLC DTC dataset (20k cells, 7 donors, 3’ v3.1 Multiplex). This part stops after ScaleData — the object you save here is the input to Tutorial 02.

Upstream script: Khushbu Patel, single-cell RNA-seq standard workflow
Reference vignette: Seurat PBMC 3k tutorial







How to use this page



The rendered HTML shows the code but does not execute it (chunks default to eval: false). To actually run the analysis:

Download the .qmd source and open it in RStudio.
Download the dataset (below) into a data/ folder at the repository root.
Work through the chunks, flipping eval: true as you go.

title: Tutorial 01 — scRNA-seq: QC & Preprocessing
text:
The first of a three-part series walking the standard Seurat scRNA-seq workflow, using the public 10x Genomics NSCLC DTC dataset (20k cells, 7 donors, 3’ v3.1 Multiplex). This part stops after ScaleData — the object you save here is the input to Tutorial 02.

Upstream script: Khushbu Patel, single-cell RNA-seq standard workflow
Reference vignette: Seurat PBMC 3k tutorial







How to use this page



The rendered HTML shows the code but does not execute it (chunks default to eval: false). To actually run the analysis:

Download the .qmd source and open it in RStudio.
Download the dataset (below) into a data/ folder at the repository root.
Work through the chunks, flipping eval: true as you go.

title: Tutorial 01 — scRNA-seq: QC & Preprocessing
text:
Dataset — 20k NSCLC DTC (10x Genomics)
What it is. Twenty-thousand dissociated tumor cells (DTCs) pooled from seven non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC) donors, run on the 10x Chromium 3’ v3.1 assay with CellPlex multiplexing (CMO hashing) so that every cell carries both a gene-expression profile and a sample-of-origin barcode.

Landing page: 10x Genomics — 20k NSCLC DTC, 3’ v3.1 Multiplex
Reference genome: GRCh38 (human)
Chemistry: 10x Chromium Single Cell 3’ v3.1 (dual index, Next GEM), CellPlex multiplexing
Cell count (target recovery): ~20,000 post-filter cells across 7 donors


Files used in this tutorial








File
Size (approx.)
What it is




20k_NSCLC_DTC_3p_nextgem_Multiplex_count_raw_feature_bc_matrix.h5
~1.5 GB
HDF5 sparse matrix of raw UMI counts: features × barcodes. Contains both Gene Expression and Multiplexing Capture assays in one file.



Download from the 10x landing page (the file sits under Output and supplemental files → Feature / cell matrix HDF5 (raw)), then place it at ../data/.


Data dictionary — Read10X_h5() return value
Read10X_h5() on this multiplex file returns a named list of sparse matrices (one per assay). Each sparse matrix has:








Slot
Type
Description




dimnames[[1]] (rows)
character
Gene symbols (e.g. MT-ND1, CD3D, KRT8). Feature-level metadata (Ensembl IDs, biotype) lives in features/ inside the HDF5 and is not returned by Read10X_h5().


dimnames[[2]] (cols)
character
16-bp 10x cell barcodes (e.g. AAACCTGAGAAACCAT-1). The -1 suffix is the GEM-well index.


values
integer
UMI counts — the number of unique transcript molecules observed for that (gene, cell) pair. Raw counts, no normalization.











Named slot in the returned list
Matrix shape
Meaning




$\Gene Expression`| \~36,600 genes × \~725,000 barcodes | The gene-expression counts. This is what the tutorial uses. | |$`Multiplexing Capture``
~12 CMOs × ~725,000 barcodes
Per-barcode CellPlex hashtag counts, used to demultiplex donors. Not used in this tutorial.









Note



The matrix is raw (all barcodes, including empty droplets). min.cells = 3 and min.features = 200 in CreateSeuratObject() apply the first-pass filters.

title: Tutorial 01 — scRNA-seq: QC & Preprocessing
text:
Learning goals
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

Read a 10x .h5 file into Seurat
Add per-cell QC metrics and visualize them
Choose filtering thresholds that are defensible for your dataset
Run NormalizeData → FindVariableFeatures → ScaleData and understand what each does

title: Tutorial 01 — scRNA-seq: QC & Preprocessing
text:
Setup

library(Seurat)
library(tidyverse)

set.seed(2026)

title: Tutorial 01 — scRNA-seq: QC & Preprocessing
text:
Step 1 — Read the 10x .h5

nsclc.sparse.m <- Read10X_h5(filename = "../data/20k_NSCLC_DTC_3p_nextgem_Multiplex_count_raw_feature_bc_matrix.h5")

# 10x multi-modal h5 files return a list; keep the Gene Expression slice
str(nsclc.sparse.m)
cts <- nsclc.sparse.m$`Gene Expression`
dim(cts)







Think about it




Why does Read10X_h5 sometimes return a list instead of a matrix?
What does each dimension of cts represent?



Show answers


10x Multiplex / CellPlex / CITE-seq / multiome runs produce multiple measurement types in one file (e.g. Gene Expression, Antibody Capture, Peaks). The reader returns a named list so you can pull the slice you want.
Rows = genes (features), columns = cell barcodes. dim(cts)[1] is the gene count, dim(cts)[2] is the cell count.

title: Tutorial 01 — scRNA-seq: QC & Preprocessing
text:
Step 2 — Create the Seurat object

nsclc.seurat.obj <- CreateSeuratObject(
  counts       = cts,
  project      = "NSCLC",
  min.cells    = 3,
  min.features = 200
)
nsclc.seurat.obj







Think about it




What exactly do min.cells = 3 and min.features = 200 remove?
The nsclc.sparse.m list still sits in memory. What would rm(nsclc.sparse.m); gc() do, and why might you want to?



Show answers


min.cells = 3 drops genes detected in fewer than 3 cells (likely noise). min.features = 200 drops barcodes with fewer than 200 detected genes — mostly empty droplets and broken cells. These are permissive first-pass filters.
It releases the raw list and its memory. The full 10x raw feature matrix is large (often several GB); once counts are inside the Seurat object, the raw list is redundant.

title: Tutorial 01 — scRNA-seq: QC & Preprocessing
text:
Step 3 — Add QC metrics

nsclc.seurat.obj[["percent.mt"]] <- PercentageFeatureSet(nsclc.seurat.obj, pattern = "^MT-")
head(nsclc.seurat.obj@meta.data)







Think about it




What biological signal does percent.mt capture?
Why "^MT-" specifically? What would you use for mouse?



Show answers


Dying or stressed cells leak cytoplasmic RNA while retaining mitochondrial RNA, so their mitochondrial fraction rises. High percent.mt flags apoptotic / compromised cells.
"^MT-" matches human mitochondrial gene symbols (MT-ND1, MT-CO1, …). For mouse the convention is lowercase: "^mt-".

title: Tutorial 01 — scRNA-seq: QC & Preprocessing
text:
Step 4 — Visualize QC

VlnPlot(nsclc.seurat.obj,
        features = c("nFeature_RNA", "nCount_RNA", "percent.mt"),
        ncol = 3)

FeatureScatter(nsclc.seurat.obj,
               feature1 = "nCount_RNA",
               feature2 = "nFeature_RNA") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")







Think about it




What does a fat tail on the nFeature_RNA violin suggest?
On the nCount_RNA vs nFeature_RNA scatter, what would a doublet look like? A dying cell?



Show answers


A long upper tail often means doublets — barcodes where two cells got trapped in one droplet, inflating both UMI count and unique gene count.
Doublets sit high on both axes, often above the main trend line. Dying cells are low on both and tend to have high percent.mt (check by coloring or faceting).

title: Tutorial 01 — scRNA-seq: QC & Preprocessing
text:
Step 5 — Filter low-quality cells

nsclc.seurat.obj <- subset(
  nsclc.seurat.obj,
  subset = nFeature_RNA > 200 &
           nFeature_RNA < 2500 &
           percent.mt   < 5
)
nsclc.seurat.obj







Think about it




The default cutoff here is percent.mt < 5. Is that appropriate for tumor tissue?
How would you set thresholds empirically rather than by default?



Show answers


Not always — tumor and certain primary tissues routinely have higher baseline percent.mt (~10–20%). A 5% cutoff can strip out viable tumor cells. Inspect the distribution first; set the knee.
Look at the violin/histogram shape; choose thresholds that trim the clear tails (not the mode). Some workshops use median ± k × MAD, or the scater::isOutlier() function for an automated rule.

title: Tutorial 01 — scRNA-seq: QC & Preprocessing
text:
Step 6 — Normalize

nsclc.seurat.obj <- NormalizeData(nsclc.seurat.obj)
# Equivalent explicit form:
# NormalizeData(nsclc.seurat.obj,
#               normalization.method = "LogNormalize",
#               scale.factor = 10000)







Think about it




What does LogNormalize do algebraically?
When would you prefer SCTransform() instead?



Show answers


For each cell: divide each gene’s count by the cell’s total count, multiply by scale.factor (default 10 000), then take log1p. In formula form: log(1 + (count_gc / total_c) * 10000).
SCTransform models the mean–variance relationship per gene with a regularized negative binomial, which is usually better for low-count datasets and more faithful downstream variance. It also replaces the NormalizeData → FindVariableFeatures → ScaleData chain in a single call.

title: Tutorial 01 — scRNA-seq: QC & Preprocessing
text:
Step 7 — Highly variable features

nsclc.seurat.obj <- FindVariableFeatures(
  nsclc.seurat.obj,
  selection.method = "vst",
  nfeatures = 2000
)

top10 <- head(VariableFeatures(nsclc.seurat.obj), 10)
plot1 <- VariableFeaturePlot(nsclc.seurat.obj)
LabelPoints(plot = plot1, points = top10, repel = TRUE)







Think about it




Why do we want to focus on variable genes instead of using all genes?
What happens if you set nfeatures = 500? Or nfeatures = 10000?



Show answers


Most genes are housekeeping / ubiquitously expressed and carry little cell-type information. Selecting HVGs raises signal-to-noise for PCA and clustering and speeds everything up.
Too few (500) risks missing subtle cell-type signals and is brittle to noisy gene choices. Too many (10 000) pulls in low-information genes and dilutes the signal. 1 500–3 000 is a common sweet spot; 2 000 is a good default.

title: Tutorial 01 — scRNA-seq: QC & Preprocessing
text:
Step 8 — Scale

all.genes <- rownames(nsclc.seurat.obj)
nsclc.seurat.obj <- ScaleData(nsclc.seurat.obj, features = all.genes)







Think about it




What does ScaleData do to each gene numerically?
Why is scaling necessary before PCA?
How would you regress out the cell cycle or percent.mt here?



Show answers


Centers each gene’s expression to mean 0 and scales to unit variance across cells (z-scoring).
PCA finds directions of maximum variance. Without scaling, highly-expressed genes (which inherently have large raw variance) would dominate PCs, regardless of whether they are biologically informative.
Pass vars.to.regress = c("percent.mt", "S.Score", "G2M.Score") (after running CellCycleScoring() for the latter two). ScaleData regresses those out of each gene’s expression before z-scoring.

title: Tutorial 01 — scRNA-seq: QC & Preprocessing
text:
Save and continue to Tutorial 02

saveRDS(nsclc.seurat.obj, file = "../data/nsclc_preprocessed.rds")







Tip



The saved .rds is the starting point for Tutorial 02 — Dimensionality reduction & clustering.

title: Tutorial 01 — scRNA-seq: QC & Preprocessing
text:
Credits

Dataset: 10x Genomics — 20k NSCLC DTC, 3’ v3.1 Multiplex
Workflow source: Khushbu Patel, single-cell RNA-seq standard workflow
Reference: Seurat PBMC 3k tutorial

title: Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression
text:
Goals of this lecture

Understand why per-cell DE between conditions is often statistically wrong
See how pseudobulk fixes it
Run a pseudobulk workflow in Seurat + DESeq2
Know when pseudobulk is and is not the right tool

title: Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression
text:
The scientific question

In cell type X, which genes differ between treated and control subjects?

title: Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression
text:
Why per-cell tests go wrong

Cells from one subject are not independent observations — they share genetics, library prep, batch, treatment
Treating each cell as an independent replicate inflates the effective N by 100×–10,000×
Result: anti-conservative p-values — thousands of spuriously “significant” genes
This has been shown repeatedly in benchmarks (Squair et al. 2021; Zimmerman et al. 2021)







Important


If your experimental unit is the subject, your statistical unit should be the subject.

title: Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression
text:
The fix in one sentence

Aggregate counts per (sample, cell type); run DESeq2 / edgeR / limma-voom on the result.

title: Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression
text: What pseudobulk does

title: Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression
text:
What changes vs. bulk RNA-seq?

Structurally — nothing. The pseudobulk matrix looks like a bulk counts matrix
You use the same tools: DESeq2, edgeR, limma-voom
The only differences are:

One matrix per cell type
Samples with too few cells in that type get dropped or flagged

title: Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression
text:
Build the pseudobulk matrix
library(Seurat)

# seu is an integrated, annotated Seurat object with:
#   seu$sample    — biological sample ID (subject / replicate)
#   seu$condition — treatment vs control
#   seu$celltype  — annotated cell type

pb <- AggregateExpression(
  seu,
  assays    = "RNA",
  group.by  = c("celltype", "sample"),
  return.seurat = FALSE
)$RNA
pb is a genes × (celltype_sample) count matrix — ready for DESeq2.

title: Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression
text:
Fit DESeq2 per cell type
library(DESeq2)

run_pb_de <- function(ct, pb_mat, sample_meta) {
  cols <- grep(paste0("^", ct, "_"), colnames(pb_mat), value = TRUE)
  mat  <- pb_mat[, cols]
  meta <- sample_meta[match(sub(paste0("^", ct, "_"), "", cols),
                            sample_meta$sample), ]

  dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(countData = mat,
                                colData   = meta,
                                design    = ~ condition)
  dds <- DESeq(dds)
  results(dds, contrast = c("condition", "treated", "control")) |> as.data.frame()
}

res_by_ct <- lapply(unique(seu$celltype),
                    run_pb_de, pb_mat = pb, sample_meta = sample_meta)
names(res_by_ct) <- unique(seu$celltype)

title: Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression
text:
edgeR and limma-voom alternatives
library(edgeR)
y <- DGEList(counts = mat, group = meta$condition)
y <- calcNormFactors(y)
design <- model.matrix(~ condition, meta)
y <- estimateDisp(y, design)
fit <- glmQLFit(y, design)
qlf <- glmQLFTest(fit, coef = 2)
topTags(qlf)
library(limma)
v   <- voom(y, design, plot = FALSE)
fit <- lmFit(v, design) |> eBayes()
topTable(fit, coef = 2, number = Inf)






Tip


All three give well-calibrated inference. DESeq2 is the most common default in the scRNA-seq literature.

title: Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression
text:
Python alternative — decoupleR + PyDESeq2
import decoupler as dc
import pydeseq2.ds as ds

pb = dc.get_pseudobulk(adata, sample_col="sample",
                       groups_col="celltype", min_cells=10, min_counts=1000)

# Then fit PyDESeq2 per celltype, contrast=treated vs control

title: Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression
text:
When to use pseudobulk

Comparing conditions across biological samples (treatment, genotype, time)
You have at least ~3 samples per group (ideally 5+)
Cell type of interest has enough cells per sample (a common rule: ~10+ cells)

title: Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression
text:
When not to use pseudobulk

Finding cluster markers for annotation — use within-cell methods (FindAllMarkers, Wilcoxon, MAST)
Only 1–2 samples per condition — no replication to pseudobulk over
Continuous traits measured per cell (e.g. pseudotime) — use models that handle the continuous covariate

title: Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression
text:
Common pitfalls

Too few cells per sample per cell type → zero-inflated pseudobulk; drop that cell type or those samples with a min_cells filter
Confounded designs: if every treated sample is on one 10x run, integration won’t rescue bad design
Ignoring covariates: include batch, sex, age in the design formula — DESeq2 will use them
Reporting single-cell N: always report N = number of samples, not number of cells, for condition-level DE

title: Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression
text:
Diagnostic plots worth running

PCA of the pseudobulk samples within a cell type — do replicates cluster by condition, or by batch?
MA / volcano plots per cell type
Hierarchical clustering of pseudobulk samples — quick confound check

title: Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression
text:
Summary

Per-cell DE between conditions → anti-conservative p-values
Pseudobulk recovers proper replication and lets you use battle-tested bulk tools
Keep per-cell methods for marker genes within a dataset, use pseudobulk for condition comparisons across subjects

title: Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression
text:
Further reading

Squair et al. 2021, Nature Communications — “Confronting false discoveries in single-cell differential expression”
Zimmerman et al. 2021, Nature Communications — “A practical solution to pseudoreplication bias in single-cell studies”
Crowell et al. 2020, Nature Communications — muscat, a pseudobulk toolkit for scRNA-seq
Heumos et al. 2023 — “Best practices for single-cell analysis across modalities”

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Overview







Pre-requisite Material


This appendix introduces the fundamental computing concepts that underlie all of the statistical and data analysis tools used in this course. Understanding hardware, operating systems, and computing architectures will help you make informed decisions about how to run your analyses.

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
What is a Computer System?



Hardware: Physical components
Software: Programs and instructions
Firmware: Low-level software in hardware
Data: Information being processed





Computer Components

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
The Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle

 B[CPU]
    B --> C[Memory]
    C --> B
    B --> D[Output Devices]
    E[Storage] --> C
    C --> E
```

## The Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle (Mermaid Original)

```dkujdatqh
graph LR
    A[Fetch Instruction] --> B[Decode Instruction]
    B --> C[Execute Instruction]
    C --> D[Store Results]
    D --> A
```
-->
Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle
Fetch: Retrieve instruction from memory
Decode: Interpret what the instruction means
Execute: Perform the operation
Store: Save results to memory or register

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Central Processing Unit (CPU)


Core Components

Control Unit (CU): Directs operations
Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU): Performs calculations
Registers: Ultra-fast temporary storage
Cache: High-speed memory buffer


Key Concepts

Clock Speed: Cycles per second (GHz)
Cores: Independent processing units
Threads: Virtual cores for parallel processing
Instruction Set: CPU’s language (x86, ARM)

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Graphics Processing Unit (GPU)


Core Components

Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs): Processing clusters
CUDA Cores/Stream Processors: Parallel compute units
Video Memory (VRAM): Dedicated high-bandwidth memory
Memory Controller: Manages data flow to/from VRAM


Key Concepts

Parallel Architecture: Thousands of cores for simultaneous tasks
Memory Bandwidth: Data throughput (GB/s)
Compute Units: Groups of processing cores
APIs: Programming interfaces (CUDA, OpenCL, Vulkan)

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
CPU vs GPU Comparison


CPU Characteristics

Design Focus: Sequential processing & complex tasks
Core Count: 4–64 powerful cores
Architecture: Optimized for single-thread performance
Best For:

Operating systems & general computing
Complex branching logic
Serial tasks



GPU Characteristics

Design Focus: Parallel processing & high throughput
Core Count: Thousands of smaller cores
Architecture: Optimized for massive parallelism
Best For:

Graphics rendering
Machine learning/AI
Scientific simulations
Parallel computations

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Random Access Memory (RAM)


Types of RAM
DRAM (Dynamic RAM)

Main system memory
Needs constant refresh
Cheaper, higher density
DDR4, DDR5 standards

SRAM (Static RAM)

Used in CPU cache
No refresh needed
Faster but expensive


Key Characteristics

Volatile: Data lost when power off
Random Access: Any location equally fast
Bandwidth: Data transfer rate
Latency: Access delay
Dual Channel: Parallel data paths

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Persistent Storage Systems


Hard Disk Drives (HDD)

Mechanical spinning platters
Magnetic storage
Large capacity, low cost
~100–200 MB/s throughput
Higher latency (~10ms)


Solid State Drives (SSD)

No moving parts (NAND Flash)
Electronic storage
Faster access (~0.1ms)
500–7000 MB/s throughput
More expensive per GB

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Input/Output (I/O) Systems


Input Devices

Keyboard & Mouse
Touchscreen
Microphone & Camera
Sensors
Network Interface


Output Devices

Monitor/Display
Printer
Speakers
Network Interface
Actuators

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
What is an Operating System?
An OS is system software that manages computer hardware, software resources, and provides common services for computer programs.


Key Roles

Resource Manager
Extended Machine
User Interface Provider
Security Enforcer


Common Operating Systems

Desktop: Windows, macOS, Unix, Linux
Mobile: Android, iOS
Server: Unix, Linux, Windows Server
Specialized: Supercomputers, IoT

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Operating System Architecture

 B[System Calls Interface]
    B --> C[Kernel]
    C --> D[Device Drivers]
    D --> E[Hardware]

    C --> F[Process Management]
    C --> G[Memory Management]
    C --> H[File System]
    C --> I[I/O Management]
```
-->
OS Architecture

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Core OS Functions


Process Management

Process Creation & Termination
Process Scheduling: CPU time allocation
Inter-Process Communication (IPC)
Synchronization
Deadlock Handling

File System Management

File Operations: Create, read, write, delete
Directory Structure
Access Control & Permissions


Memory Management

Memory Allocation/Deallocation
Virtual Memory
Paging & Segmentation
Memory Protection
Swap Space Management

Device Management

Device Drivers: Hardware abstraction
I/O Scheduling
Buffering & Caching
Interrupt Handling

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
CPU Scheduling


Scheduling Algorithms

First-Come-First-Served (FCFS): Simple but can cause long waits
Shortest Job First (SJF): Optimal average wait time
Round Robin (RR): Fair time slices for all
Priority Scheduling: Important tasks first


Performance Metrics

CPU Utilization: Keep CPU busy
Throughput: Jobs completed/time
Turnaround Time: Total completion time
Waiting Time: Time in ready queue
Response Time: First response delay

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Security & Protection


Protection Mechanisms

Access Control Lists (ACLs)
User/Group Permissions
Memory Protection: Segmentation
CPU Modes: User vs Kernel
Sandboxing: Isolate processes


Security Features

Authentication: User verification
Encryption: Data protection
Firewall: Network protection
Updates: Patch vulnerabilities



“Every program and user should operate using the least amount of privilege necessary”

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Computing Timeline

 B[1980s-90sComputing TimelinePersonal Computers]
    B --> C[1990s-2000sClient-Server]
    C --> D[2000sClusters/Grids]
    D --> E[2006+Cloud Computing]
    E --> F[2020sEdge/Hybrid]
```
-->
Key Drivers of Change

Performance Needs: Growing computational demands
Economics: Cost optimization and economies of scale
Connectivity: Internet bandwidth improvements
Virtualization: Hardware abstraction technologies

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Local Computing Overview







Definition


Computing resources that are physically present and directly controlled by the user or organization






Characteristics

Complete control over hardware
Data stays on-premises
No network dependency for compute
Predictable performance
One-time purchase model


Common Setups

Desktop workstations
Laptop computers
On-premises servers
Local development machines

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Local Computing Architecture

 B[Operating System]
    B --> C[Hardware Abstraction Layer]
    C --> D[CPU]
    C --> E[Memory]
    C --> F[Storage]
    C --> G[GPU]
    C --> H[Network Card]
    H --> I[Internet/LAN]
```
-->
Local Computing Architecture

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Local Computing: Pros and Cons


Advantages

Low Latency: No network overhead
Data Control: Complete ownership
Privacy: Data never leaves premises
Predictable Costs: One-time investment
Offline Capability: Works without internet
Customization: Full hardware/software control


Disadvantages

Limited Scale: Hardware constraints
Maintenance: User responsibility
Upfront Costs: High initial investment
Disaster Recovery: Manual backup needed
Resource Waste: Idle during off-hours
Upgrade Complexity: Physical intervention

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Cluster Computing Overview
A collection of interconnected computers working together as a single system

 N1[Node 1]
    M --> N2[Node 2]
    M --> N3[Node 3]
    M --> N4[Node N]
    N1 -.-> S[Shared Storage]
    N2 -.-> S
    N3 -.-> S
    N4 -.-> S
    U[Users] --> M
```
-->
Cluster ComputingKey Components

Head Node: Job scheduling and management
Compute Nodes: Worker machines
Interconnect: High-speed network (InfiniBand, 10GbE+)
Shared Storage: Parallel file systems (Lustre, GPFS)

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Resource Managers
These are used to schedule computing jobs on computer clusters.



System
Use Case
Key Features




SLURM
HPC
Advanced scheduling, power management


PBS/Torque
Traditional HPC
Mature, stable, wide support


SGE/UGE
Mixed workloads
Flexible policies


Kubernetes
Containers
Microservices, auto-scaling










Note


We use SLURM on Talapas at the University of Oregon for scheduling compute jobs. See Appendix F for more details on using Talapas.

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Cluster Computing: Pros and Cons


Advantages

Scalability: Add nodes as needed
Performance: Parallel processing power
Cost-Effective: Commodity hardware
Fault Tolerance: Node redundancy
Resource Sharing: Multiple users/projects
Specialized Hardware: GPUs, high-mem nodes


Disadvantages

Complexity: Requires expertise
Infrastructure: Space, power, cooling
Network Dependency: Interconnect bottlenecks
Software Licenses: Per-node costs
Maintenance: Hardware failures
Initial Setup: Complex configuration

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Cloud Computing Overview
On-demand delivery of computing resources over the internet with pay-as-you-go pricing

 AZ1[Availability Zone 1]
    R --> AZ2[Availability Zone 2]
    AZ1 --> C[Compute]
    AZ1 --> S[Storage]
    AZ1 --> N[Networking]
    AZ2 --> C2[Compute]
    AZ2 --> S2[Storage]
    AZ2 --> N2[Networking]
    U[Users] -.Internet.-> R
```
-->
Cloud Computing ArchitectureMajor Providers

Amazon Web Services (AWS): Market leader, 200+ services
Microsoft Azure: Enterprise integration
Google Cloud Platform: AI/ML focus

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Cloud Computing: Pros and Cons


Advantages

Elastic Scaling: Instant resource adjustment
Pay-per-Use: No idle resource costs
Global Reach: Multiple regions
Managed Services: Reduced operations
Innovation Speed: Latest technologies
Disaster Recovery: Built-in redundancy


Disadvantages

Ongoing Costs: Can exceed on-prem
Vendor Lock-in: Proprietary services
Internet Dependency: Connectivity required
Data Transfer Costs: Egress fees
Compliance Complexity: Data residency
Less Control: Shared responsibility
Performance Variability: Noisy neighbors

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
section: Why Do We Need Coding and Scripting?
text:
Why Do We Need Coding and Scripting?

It is incredibly fast and powerful, particularly for repeated actions
It allows you to do thousands of ‘clicks’ with single commands
Ability to analyze large datasets that Excel and other GUIs can’t handle well
Access to thousands of free programs made for and by scientists
The commands work almost identically across platforms
Ability to use computer clusters like Talapas

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Coding vs. Scripting

coding generally involves computer languages that use compilers

C++, Fortran, Basic, etc.

scripting generally involves computer languages that are interpreted on the fly

Unix, Python, R, Julia, etc.

Coding — faster but less flexible; Scripting — flexible but slower
The distinction has become somewhat fuzzy and most modern analytical pipelines contain a combination of both

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Computational Tools for This Course







What you will learn in these appendices



What is local, cluster, and cloud computing (this lecture)
Unix paths, commands, and scripts (Appendix B & C)
Read and write basic scripts in R (Appendix D)
Git and GitHub (Appendix E)
Talapas and High-Performance Computing (Appendix F)

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Overview







Pre-requisite Material


This appendix covers the Unix command line — the foundational interface for scientific computing. You will learn shell navigation, file operations, reading and writing files, I/O redirection, pipes, and how to work with large data files. See also Week 1 of the main lectures for an introduction to these concepts.

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
What is Unix?

A scripting language developed in 1969, released in 1973
Serves as the base language for many programs and computers
Runs both locally on your computer and on large clusters like Talapas
Linux is an open-source version of the same language

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
What is a Shell?

The ‘shell’ is a program that runs UNIX and takes in commands and gives them to the operating system
Bash acts as the shell in Macs, Linux, and now Windows
You can access the shell via a terminal window

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
section: Accessing the Shell — Mac and Linux
text:
Accessing the Shell — Mac and Linux

Mac users: open the “Terminal” app, or use another app like ‘iTerm2’
Linux users: open one of several “Terminal” apps








Windows users have a little more work to do


See the next slides for Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) installation

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Accessing the Shell — Windows

Guide: https://ubuntu.com/tutorials/install-ubuntu-on-wsl2-on-windows-10
Run Windows PowerShell as administrator
Install WSL2 by typing wsl --install
Restart your computer
Search for and install Ubuntu from Microsoft store app
OR type wsl --install -d ubuntu on PowerShell to do both at once

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Setting Up WSL

Open Ubuntu and set up a username and password
Does not have to match your login info for Windows
Run sudo apt update then sudo apt upgrade to ensure everything is up to date
Will need to create folders and files within your Ubuntu folder on your computer

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Recipes for a Shell Command

Prompt: notation used to indicate your computer is ready to accept a new command
Command: the building blocks of programming, tell computer to do a specific task
Options: change the behavior of a command
Argument: what the command should operate on

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
How is a Computer Organized?

System of directories (folders) and files
/ = the root directory, which holds all other directories
Most of your files will be located under /Users in a directory of your username
The ~ is shorthand for your home folder
Navigation in the shell consists of jumping up and down between directories
The “path” refers to the location a file is in (e.g., /Users/wcresko/Documents)

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text: The Unix File Hierarchy

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Relative vs. Absolute Paths

ConceptExamples



Absolute path: Full path from root / (e.g., /Users/wcresko/Documents)
Relative path: Path from current location (e.g., ../Documents)
. refers to the current directory
.. refers to the parent directory



# Absolute path
cd /Users/wcresko/Documents

# Relative paths
cd ..           # Go up one directory
cd ./data       # Go into data directory (same as cd data)
cd ../scripts   # Go up one, then into scripts
cd ~            # Go to home directory
cd -            # Go to previous directory

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Common Navigation Commands

CommandsPractice



pwd = “print working directory” — shows where you are
ls = “list” — list all directories and files in current position

ls -F = denote which results are directories, files, etc.
ls -l = “long format” — lists total file sizes
ls -r = “reverse” — lists the results in reverse order
ls -S = “size” — sort results by size
ls -t = “time” — sort results by time created

cd = “change directory”

cd .. = go up one directory
cd - = go to last directory
cd ~ = go to home directory




Try navigating around your computer using cd and ls:
# Where am I?
pwd

# What's in my home directory?
cd ~
ls -la

# Move into Documents
cd Documents
ls

# Go back up
cd ..
pwd

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
File Operations

CommandsImportant Notes



Make new folders: mkdir
Make new files: nano, touch
Rename files: mv
Move files: mv
Copy files: cp
Delete files: rm
Delete directories: rmdir
Examining file length: wc
Reading files: cat
Looking at beginning or end: head or tail




The shell trusts you

It will delete files you say to delete
It will override files if you name 2 things the same

Naming conventions

Avoid spaces
Don’t start with a –
Stick to letters, numbers, ., -, and _

Use appropriate file extensions in file names

Some software expect files with certain extensions (.fasta, .txt, etc.)

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Practice: File Operations







Hands-On Exercise



Make a new directory called whatever you’d like
Add a file named Practice.txt to the directory and add some text
Read the contents of the file and get its length
Rename the file to Super_practice.txt
Move the file to a new folder named Testing
Make a copy named Super_practice_copy.txt
Delete the original Super_practice.txt

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Commands for Reading Files

OverviewExamples



cat = concatenate and display files (good for small files)
less = view file page by page (space forward, b back, q quit, /pattern search)
more = similar to less but simpler
head = display first 10 lines (default); head -n 20 file.txt for first 20
tail = display last 10 lines (default); tail -f file.txt follows a growing file



# View entire small file
cat small_file.txt

# Page through a large file
less large_file.txt

# First and last lines
head -n 5 data.txt
tail -n 5 data.txt

# Follow a log file in real time
tail -f logfile.txt

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Input Redirection

ConceptExamples



< = redirect input from a file (command < input.txt)
<< = here document (multi-line input)
Example uses:

wc -l < data.txt counts lines in data.txt
sort < names.txt sorts contents of names.txt
Many commands can read from files directly OR from stdin




# Count lines using redirection
wc -l < data.txt

# Sort file contents
sort < names.txt

# Word count
wc -w < report.txt

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Output Redirection

OperatorsExamples



> = redirect output to a file (overwrites!)
>> = append output to a file
2> = redirect error messages
&> = redirect both output and errors



# Save file listing to a file (overwrites)
ls -l > filelist.txt

# Append to existing file
echo "new line" >> existing.txt

# Capture errors separately
command 2> errors.txt

# Capture everything
command &> all_output.txt

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Unix Three Standard Streams

ConceptDiagramRedirection



stdin (0) = Standard Input (default: keyboard)
stdout (1) = Standard Output (default: terminal screen)
stderr (2) = Standard Error (default: terminal screen)



         ┌──────────────┐
stdin ───>│              │───> stdout
    (0)  │   Program    │      (1)
         │              │───> stderr
         └──────────────┘      (2)

File descriptor numbers: 0, 1, 2
Can redirect each stream independently
Programs don’t know if streams are redirected
This abstraction is key to Unix philosophy



# Redirect stdout to file (equivalent ways)
ls -l > files.txt
ls -l 1> files.txt

# Redirect stderr to file
ls /nonexistent 2> errors.txt

# Redirect both to same file
ls -l /nonexistent &> all_output.txt

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
What are Pipes?

ConceptVisual



| = the pipe operator

Takes output from one command as input to another
Chains commands together
No intermediate files needed!

Basic syntax: command1 | command2 | command3
Power of Unix philosophy:

Small tools that do one thing well
Combine them to do complex tasks

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Common Pipe Patterns

Basic PatternsAdvanced PatternsFull Pipeline



Counting patterns: grep "pattern" file.txt | wc -l
Sorting and uniqueness: cat file.txt | sort | uniq
Finding top/bottom items: sort data.txt | head -5
Filtering and processing: ls -l | grep ".txt" | awk '{print $5, $9}'



# Extract column 2, count unique values, sort by frequency
cat data.csv | cut -d',' -f2 | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

# Find all txt files and sort by line count
find . -name "*.txt" | xargs wc -l | sort -n

# Watch log file for errors in real-time
tail -f logfile.txt | grep ERROR


# Read a file, process it, save results, and display
cat data.txt | \
  grep -v "^#" | \       # Remove comment lines
  cut -f1,3 | \          # Extract columns 1 and 3
  sort -k2 -n | \        # Sort by column 2 numerically
  tee results.txt | \    # Save to file AND pass along
  head -10               # Show top 10

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Bioinformatics Pipeline Example

PipelineTips


# FASTQ processing pipeline with error handling
zcat reads.fastq.gz 2> unzip_errors.log |
fastqc stdin 2> qc_errors.log |
trimmomatic 2> trim_errors.log |
bowtie2 -x genome - 2> alignment_stats.txt |
samtools sort 2> sort_errors.log |
samtools index - 2> index_errors.log

# Check all error logs at once
cat *_errors.log | grep -E "ERROR|WARNING"



Test pipes step by step — build complex pipes incrementally
Check output at each stage
Use less for large files instead of cat
Remember: > overwrites, >> appends
Filter early in the pipeline to reduce data passed between commands
Save intermediate results when debugging complex pipes

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Practice Exercise: Files and Pipes







Hands-On Exercise



Create a file with a list of numbers (one per line)
Use pipes to sort them numerically
Save the sorted list to a new file using redirection
Use tee to display and save the top 5 numbers
Count how many unique numbers you have using pipes
Append the count to your results file

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Human Chromosome Data

Genomic data files can be massive (human genome ~6 billion base pairs)
FASTA format is standard for sequence data
Perfect use case for Unix pipes and redirection
Let’s work with human genome assembly version 38 (~3.1 GB)

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Downloading Genomic Data

wgetcurlNotes


# Using wget to download human genome
wget https://ftp.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/genomes/all/GCF/000/001/405/\
GCF_000001405.40_GRCh38.p14/GCF_000001405.40_GRCh38.p14_genomic.fna.gz


# Using curl and save with specific name
curl -o human_genome.fa.gz https://ftp.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/genomes/all/GCF/000/001/405/\
GCF_000001405.40_GRCh38.p14/GCF_000001405.40_GRCh38.p14_genomic.fna.gz



wget and curl are both tools for downloading files
The backslash (\) allows long URLs to span multiple lines
.gz extension means the file is compressed

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Examining Large Files Efficiently

CommandsKey Rule


# Decompress and look at first 10 lines
gunzip -c human_genome.fa.gz | head -10

# Check file size before and after decompression
ls -lh human_genome.fa.gz
gunzip human_genome.fa.gz
ls -lh human_genome.fa

# Count sequences in FASTA file (lines starting with >)
grep "^>" human_genome.fa | wc -l

# View sequence headers only
grep "^>" human_genome.fa | less









Don’t use cat on huge files!


Use head, tail, less, or streaming tools instead. Loading a 3 GB file into memory with cat will slow down or crash your session.

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Processing FASTA Files with Pipes

Extract and CountStream Processing


# Extract just the sequence (no headers) and count bases
grep -v "^>" human_genome.fa | tr -d '\n' | wc -c

# Count each type of nucleotide
grep -v "^>" human_genome.fa | \
  tr -d '\n' | \
  fold -w1 | \
  sort | \
  uniq -c


# Download, decompress, and process in one pipeline
curl -s https://url/to/genome.fa.gz | \
  gunzip -c | \
  grep -v "^>" | \
  tr -d '\n' | \
  cut -c1-1000000 > first_megabase.txt

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Working with Compressed Files

View without ExtractingCompress/Decompress


# View compressed files without extracting
zcat file.gz | head
zless file.gz
zgrep "pattern" file.gz


# Compress and decompress
gzip file.txt          # Creates file.txt.gz
gunzip file.txt.gz     # Restores file.txt
gzip -k file.txt       # Keep original file

# Concatenate compressed files
zcat file1.gz file2.gz | gzip > combined.gz

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Key Takeaways







Unix Best Practices for Large Data



Real bioinformatics often involves gigabytes of data — these Unix tools scale beautifully
Never load entire genome files into memory — use streaming
Combine tools — each does one thing well
Test on subsets first — extract small portions for development
Document your pipelines — save commands in scripts
Use compression — work with .gz files directly when possible
Think in streams — data flows through pipes without intermediate files

title: Appendix B - Unix & the Command Line
text:
Where Do You Get Help?

Manual pages!

The shell has manuals for all basic commands
Type man [command_name] to access the manual for a specific command
Type q to exit

Also… the internet!
Also… Generative AI (ChatGPT, Claude, etc.)

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Goals of this lecture

Understand what bulk RNA-seq measures vs. what single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) measures
Walk the full scRNA-seq pipeline end-to-end at a conceptual level
Meet the common tools at each step, with a Seurat focus and scanpy alternatives
Preview key downstream analyses: annotation, DE, enrichment, WGCNA, pseudobulk, trajectory, cell-cell communication







Note


Theme for the workshop: what is the same vs. what is new when we measure expression per cell instead of per tissue.

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Bulk RNA-seq measures the average

mRNA from many cells is pooled, reverse-transcribed, sequenced
You get one expression profile per sample (usually ~20,000 genes × N samples)
Excellent for: comparing tissues / treatments / genotypes in well-controlled designs with biological replicates







Tip


Bulk is cheap, mature, and statistically well-understood. It remains the right tool for many questions.

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
What bulk cannot tell you

How many cell types are in the tissue, and in what proportions
Whether a gene is “up” because it changed within a cell type, or because the cell-type composition shifted
Rare populations, transitional states, developmental trajectories
Cell-cell communication in situ

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Bulk vs. single-cell — the picture


Bulk: one profile per sample
Single-cell: one profile per cell — heterogeneity becomes visible

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Commonalities you can carry over

Same core measurement: count of reads/UMIs per gene
Same statistical concerns: normalization, batch effects, multiple testing, replication
Many downstream questions (DE, enrichment, co-expression) are the same questions at a new resolution

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
What is genuinely new

Sparsity: ~90–95% zeros in typical droplet data (not all real biology — technical dropout too)
Cells are not replicates: cells from one subject are not statistically independent
Cell type is a latent variable you have to recover from the data
Integration across samples is a first-class problem, not an afterthought

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Major scRNA-seq technologies









Category
Examples
Strengths
Trade-offs




Plate-based, full-length
Smart-seq2/3
Full transcript coverage, isoforms, high sensitivity
Low throughput, more expensive per cell


Droplet-based, 3’/5’
10x Genomics Chromium, Drop-seq, inDrops
High throughput (1k–100k+ cells)
3’ or 5’ only, shallower per cell


Combinatorial barcoding
Parse Evercode, SPLiT-seq
No specialized instrument, fixable samples
Protocol complexity


Nuclei (snRNA-seq)
10x Nuclei
Works on frozen / difficult tissues
Fewer transcripts, no cytoplasmic RNA









Note


10x Chromium 3’ is the workshop default. Most conceptual ideas generalize.

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
section: How we got here — a short timeline
text:
How we got here — a short timeline


Throughput went from one cell (Tang 2009) to millions of cells in a decade
The field now extends beyond dissociated scRNA-seq into multi-modal and spatial measurements

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Droplet protocol: barcodes + UMIs


Cell barcode identifies which cell a read came from
UMI identifies the original mRNA molecule (collapses PCR duplicates)
Reads are pooled and demultiplexed computationally

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
section: From reads to a count matrix
text:
From reads to a count matrix

Input: raw FASTQ files (R1 = cell BC + UMI, R2 = transcript)
Output: cells × genes count matrix (integer UMI counts)
Common tools:

Cell Ranger (10x, official)
STARsolo (STAR-based, fast, flexible)
alevin-fry / salmon (selective alignment, very fast)
kb-python (kallisto | bustools) (pseudoalignment)

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Primary-processing pipelines side-by-side


Three common entry points: 10x Chromium, Drop-seq / inDrops, bulk or SMART-based
Different stacks of tools, same destination: a counts matrix that Seurat / scanpy can read

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
The data structure you analyze

Sparse matrix: rows = genes, columns = cells (or transposed in Python)
Paired with per-cell metadata (sample, batch, QC metrics) and per-gene metadata


R / SeuratPython / scanpy


library(Seurat)
counts <- Read10X(data.dir = "filtered_feature_bc_matrix/")
seu    <- CreateSeuratObject(counts = counts, project = "workshop",
                             min.cells = 3, min.features = 200)
seu


import scanpy as sc
adata = sc.read_10x_mtx("filtered_feature_bc_matrix/", var_names="gene_symbols")
sc.pp.filter_cells(adata, min_genes=200)
sc.pp.filter_genes(adata, min_cells=3)
adata

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text: Bulk RNA-seq workflow (for comparison)

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text: scRNA-seq pipeline at a glance

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
section: Same data, two complementary analysis chains
text:
Same data, two complementary analysis chains


Bulk path: DE + WGCNA → markers related to conditions → enrichment / networks
scRNA-seq path: clustering + annotation → markers per cluster → enrichment
You can (and often do) run both on the same data — for scRNA-seq, the bulk path runs on the pseudobulk matrix

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
section: The Seurat workflow in eight steps
text: The Seurat workflow in eight steps

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
The eight steps, in words

QC & Filter — create the Seurat object, drop low-quality cells and low-expression genes
Normalization & Scaling — LogNormalize then ScaleData, optionally regressing out percent.mt, cell-cycle, etc.
Feature Extraction — FindVariableFeatures (VST) → ~2000 highly variable genes
Dimension Reduction — RunPCA, then ElbowPlot / JackStraw to pick N PCs
Cell Clustering — FindNeighbors → FindClusters; use clustree to pick a resolution; then RunUMAP / RunTSNE
Marker Identification — FindAllMarkers + visualizations; gene-set enrichment on the markers
Trajectory Inference — Monocle / Slingshot / PAGA / scVelo for continuous processes
Cell-type Annotation — RenameIdents from markers + SingleR / Azimuth / CellTypist for automation

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Same shape, different resolution

Same three big lanes: wet lab → preprocessing → analysis
Bulk stops at a sample counts matrix; scRNA-seq starts with a cell counts matrix and gains integration, clustering, annotation before “classical” DE
Many classical downstream steps (enrichment, WGCNA) work on pseudobulk derived from scRNA-seq, so the bulk skills transfer directly

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Step 1 — Per-cell QC


nFeature_RNA: genes detected per cell
nCount_RNA: total UMI counts per cell
percent.mt: % reads mapping to mitochondrial genes (stress/dying cells)

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
QC in practice

Seuratscanpy


seu[["percent.mt"]] <- PercentageFeatureSet(seu, pattern = "^MT-")
VlnPlot(seu, features = c("nFeature_RNA", "nCount_RNA", "percent.mt"), ncol = 3)
seu <- subset(seu, nFeature_RNA > 200 & nFeature_RNA < 6000 & percent.mt < 15)


adata.var["mt"] = adata.var_names.str.startswith("MT-")
sc.pp.calculate_qc_metrics(adata, qc_vars=["mt"], inplace=True, percent_top=None)
adata = adata[(adata.obs.n_genes_by_counts > 200) &
              (adata.obs.n_genes_by_counts < 6000) &
              (adata.obs.pct_counts_mt < 15)].copy()









Warning


Thresholds are dataset-specific. Always look at the distributions first.

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
section: Droplets can also be empty — or contaminated
text:
Droplets can also be empty — or contaminated


Many barcodes correspond to empty droplets with only ambient RNA — filter with EmptyDrops or the Cell Ranger knee-point
Even real cells are contaminated by ambient RNA soup; estimate and subtract with SoupX, DecontX, or CellBender

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Step 2 — Doublet detection

Droplets sometimes capture two cells → artificial “hybrid” profiles
Run doublet detection on each sample before integration





Tool
Ecosystem
Notes




scDblFinder
R / Bioc
Fast, recommended default


DoubletFinder
R
Widely cited, parameter tuning


Scrublet
Python
Classic choice in scanpy flows

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Step 3 — Normalization

Goal: put cells on a comparable scale before downstream math
Two dominant approaches:

Log-normalization (NormalizeData / sc.pp.normalize_total + log1p): fast, simple
Variance-stabilizing (SCTransform in Seurat, scran::sctransform): models mean-variance, often better for low-count cells


# Seurat
seu <- NormalizeData(seu) |>
  FindVariableFeatures(nfeatures = 2000) |>
  ScaleData()

# Alternative
seu <- SCTransform(seu, verbose = FALSE)
# scanpy
sc.pp.normalize_total(adata, target_sum=1e4)
sc.pp.log1p(adata)
sc.pp.highly_variable_genes(adata, n_top_genes=2000)

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Step 4 — Feature selection (HVGs)

Use the ~1,000–3,000 most variable genes for downstream geometry
Dramatically reduces noise from ubiquitously-expressed housekeeping genes
Seurat: FindVariableFeatures(); scanpy: sc.pp.highly_variable_genes()

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Step 5 — Linear dimensionality reduction (PCA)

PCA on scaled HVG expression gives a low-dimensional, denoised representation
Typical: first 20–50 PCs used for all downstream neighbor-graph steps
Choose the number of PCs with an ElbowPlot() or JackStraw() / variance explained curve

seu <- RunPCA(seu, npcs = 50)
ElbowPlot(seu, ndims = 50)

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text: Step 6 — Integration / batch correction

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Integration tools








Tool
Ecosystem
Approach




Harmony
R / Py
Iterative soft-clustering in PC space


Seurat CCA / RPCA
R
Anchor-based cross-sample matching


scVI / scANVI
Python
Variational autoencoder


BBKNN
Python
Batch-balanced k-NN graph


MNN / fastMNN
R / Py
Mutual nearest neighbors









Tip


Rule of thumb: try a simple method (Harmony) first; move to scVI/scANVI for very large or heterogeneous atlases.

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Step 7 — Neighborhood graph, clustering, embedding

Build a shared nearest-neighbor (SNN) graph from the integrated PCs
Cluster with Louvain or (preferred today) Leiden at a chosen resolution
Embed for visualization with UMAP or t-SNE

seu <- FindNeighbors(seu, dims = 1:30) |>
  FindClusters(resolution = 0.5, algorithm = 4) |> # 4 = Leiden
  RunUMAP(dims = 1:30)
DimPlot(seu, reduction = "umap", label = TRUE)
sc.pp.neighbors(adata, n_pcs=30)
sc.tl.leiden(adata, resolution=0.5)
sc.tl.umap(adata)
sc.pl.umap(adata, color="leiden", legend_loc="on data")






Warning


UMAP distances between clusters are not quantitative. Use for visualization, not inference.

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text: Two paths, use both

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Manual annotation — marker-driven

Find cluster markers

markers <- FindAllMarkers(seu, only.pos = TRUE, min.pct = 0.25,
                          logfc.threshold = 0.25)
markers |> group_by(cluster) |> slice_max(avg_log2FC, n = 10)

Compare to canonical markers for the tissue

CellMarker 2.0, PanglaoDB, tissue-specific references

Assign labels; RenameIdents() / seu$celltype <- ...

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Automatic annotation — reference-driven

Fast, reproducible, reduces personal bias
Always sanity-check against markers




Tool
Reference / mechanism




SingleR
Bulk/sc references, correlation-based


Azimuth
Seurat v4 reference mapping (human refs)


CellTypist
Logistic regression models, many tissues (py)


scType
Marker-list–based scoring


ProjecTILs
T-cell / TME specialized









Tip


Best practice: run an automatic method and confirm with manual marker inspection. Disagreements often flag interesting biology.

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
section: The annotated object is a launchpad
text: The annotated object is a launchpad

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
DE between conditions

Question: within a given cell type, which genes change with treatment / genotype / time?
Do NOT treat cells as biological replicates — they are not independent
Preferred approach: pseudobulk (covered in its own lecture)
Within-cell methods still useful for exploratory cluster markers: FindMarkers, MAST, rank_genes_groups

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Enrichment / pathway analysis

Summarize gene lists into interpretable biology
Main flavors:

Over-representation (hypergeometric): enrichR, clusterProfiler
Rank-based GSEA: fgsea, clusterProfiler’s gseGO/gseKEGG
Per-cell scoring: decoupleR, AUCell, GSVA, AddModuleScore


# per-cell pathway activity (module scoring)
seu <- AddModuleScore(seu, features = list(interferon_genes), name = "IFN")
FeaturePlot(seu, "IFN1")

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Co-expression modules (WGCNA)


hdWGCNA (Morabito et al.) adapts WGCNA to sparse single-cell data using metacells
Alternative: build pseudobulk matrices per cell type, then run classic WGCNA
Output: gene modules, module eigengenes, module-trait correlations, hub genes

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Trajectory / pseudotime

For continuous processes (development, differentiation, activation)
Tools: Monocle3, Slingshot, PAGA, scVelo (RNA velocity)
Requires biological justification — not every dataset has a trajectory

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Cell–cell communication

Infer signaling between cell types from ligand-receptor expression
Tools: CellChat, LIANA (meta-resource), NicheNet, CellPhoneDB

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Pseudobulk — preview

Aggregate single-cell counts to one value per (sample, cell type)
Hand the resulting matrix to DESeq2 / edgeR / limma-voom
Gives properly calibrated DE with real biological replication
Covered in its own lecture next

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
section: The landscape is wider than scRNA-seq alone
text: The landscape is wider than scRNA-seq alone

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Why spatial matters

Dissociation discards tissue architecture. scRNA-seq tells you what cells are present, not where they are
Spatial transcriptomics recovers tissue context at varying resolutions (spot → subcellular)
Same downstream concepts apply: QC, normalization, clustering, annotation — plus new ones (cell2location, neighborhood enrichment)
Often used in combination: scRNA-seq as a reference for deconvolving lower-resolution spatial data

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
What to remember

scRNA-seq is the same measurement at higher resolution — count-based, but per cell
Sparsity + lack of cell-level independence change the statistics
The canonical pipeline: QC → normalize → HVGs → PCA → integrate → cluster → annotate → downstream
Seurat in R and scanpy in Python cover the same steps with different idioms — pick one, learn it well, be fluent enough to read the other
For condition-level DE, default to pseudobulk

title: Lecture 01 — Bulk vs. Single-Cell RNA-seq: Overview of the Pipeline
text:
Further reading

Heumos et al. 2023, Nature Reviews Genetics — “Best practices for single-cell analysis across modalities”
Luecken & Theis 2019, Molecular Systems Biology — “Current best practices in single-cell RNA-seq analysis”
Amezquita et al. 2020 — Orchestrating Single-Cell Analysis with Bioconductor (OSCA)
Seurat tutorials · Scanpy tutorials · sc-best-practices book

title: Appendix D - R Programming Fundamentals
text:
Overview







Pre-requisite Material


This appendix provides a comprehensive introduction to the R programming language. R is the primary tool used throughout this course for statistical analysis and data visualization. See also Week 1 of the main lectures for additional R and RStudio basics.

title: Appendix D - R Programming Fundamentals
text:
Why Use R?

Good general scripting tool for statistics and mathematics
Powerful, flexible, and free
Runs on all computer platforms
New enhancements coming out all the time
Superb data management & graphics capabilities
Reproducibility — keep your scripts to see exactly what was done
You can write your own functions
Lots of online help available
Can use a nice GUI front end such as RStudio
Can embed your R analyses in dynamic, polished files using Quarto Markdown

title: Appendix D - R Programming Fundamentals
text:
R Scripts and Quarto Markdown

Often we want to write scripts that can just be run — R scripts
We can also embed code in Quarto Markdown files for more annotations
This improves interpretability and reproducibility
You can insert R code chunks into Quarto Markdown documents
A series of R commands that will be executed
Can add comments using hashtags #
Can have pipes (|>) to connect one step to the next

Basic Math in R

OutputCodeInterpretation




[1] 16

4 * 4
(4 + 3 * 2^2)




Commands can be submitted through the terminal, console, or scripts
R follows the normal priority of mathematical evaluation (PEMDAS)
Parentheses control order of operations just as in algebra

text:
Assigning Variables

OutputCodeInterpretation




[1] 6


[1] 4





x <- 2
x * 3
y <- x * 3
y - 2




Variables are assigned values using the <- operator
Variable names must begin with a letter
R is case sensitive (x and X are different)
The result of calculations can be assigned to new variables
Names like 3y <- 3 or 3*y <- 3 will not work

text:
Arithmetic Operations and Functions

OutputCodeInterpretation




[1] 67


[1] 69022864





# Common math functions
x + 2
x^2
log(x)    # natural log (built-in function)
sqrt(x)

# Assigning results
y <- 67
print(y)

z <- (x * y)^2

log is a built-in function — the object goes in parentheses
Functions can take arguments that modify behavior (e.g., log(x, base = 10))
The outcome of calculations can be assigned to new variables
Use print() to display values explicitly

Strings

OutputCodeInterpretation

x <- "I Love"
print(x)
y <- "Bioengineering"
print(y)
z <- c(x, y)    # c stands for concatenate

Characters need to be set off by quotation marks
The c() function stands for concatenate
Reusing variable names overwrites previous assignments
Good practice: use descriptive, unique names for each variable

text:
Vectors

OutputCodeInterpretation

# Operations work element-wise

print(y)

# Plot vectors
plot(x, y)




R thinks in terms of vectors (a list of values)
Vectors can be assigned directly using the c() function
Mathematical operations on vectors work element-wise
Writing programs with vectors in mind will simplify most things

Getting Help in R

MethodsInterpretation


help(mean)           # Open help page
?mean                # Same as help()
example(mean)        # Show examples

Drawing Random Samples

OutputCodeInterpretation














# Draw from normal distribution
x <- rnorm(10000, 0, 10)
hist(x)

# Draw uniform random integers
y <- sample(1:10000, 10000, replace = TRUE)

# Combine and plot
xy <- cbind(x, y)
plot(x, y)




rnorm(n, mean, sd) draws n random values from a normal distribution

Overlaying Density Curves

OutputCodeInterpretation














x <- rnorm(1000, 0, 100)
hist(x, xlim = c(-500, 500), probability = TRUE)
curve(dnorm(x, 0, 100), xlim = c(-500, 500), add = TRUE, col = 'red', lwd = 2)

Creating Data Frames

OutputCodeInterpretation




         hydrogel_concentration compression conductivity
Sample_1                    low         3.4          0.0

Sample_3                   high         8.4          3.8
Sample_4                   high         3.0          5.0

Sample_6                 medium         8.1          4.1

# Set up vectors as columns
hydrogel_concentration <- factor(c("low", "high", "high", "high",
                                    "medium", "medium", "medium", "low"))
compression <- c(3.4, 3.4, 8.4, 3, 5.6, 8.1, 8.3, 4.5)
conductivity <- c(0, 9.2, 3.8, 5, 5.6, 4.1, 7.1, 5.3)

# Create data frame
mydata <- data.frame(hydrogel_concentration, compression, conductivity)
row.names(mydata) <- c("Sample_1", "Sample_2", "Sample_3", "Sample_4",
                        "Sample_5", "Sample_6", "Sample_7", "Sample_8")
print(mydata)




Data frames are the primary data structure for tabular data in R
Vectors become columns when combined with data.frame()
factor() creates categorical variables with fixed levels
row.names() assigns labels to each row

title: Appendix D - R Programming Fundamentals
text:
Reading and Writing Data

ReadingWritingInterpretation


# Comma-separated files
YourFile <- read.table('yourfile.csv', header = TRUE, row.names = 1, sep = ',')
# OR use the convenience function:
YourFile <- read.csv('yourfile.csv')

# Tab-separated files
YourFile <- read.table('yourfile.txt', header = TRUE, row.names = 1, sep = '\t')

# Tidyverse versions (return tibbles)
YourFile <- read_csv('yourfile.csv')
YourFile <- read_tsv('yourfile.txt')

# Excel files
library(readxl)
YourFile <- read_excel('yourfile.xlsx')


# Write to CSV
write.table(YourFile, "yourfile.csv", quote = FALSE, row.names = TRUE, sep = ",")
# OR:
write.csv(YourFile, "yourfile.csv")

# Write to TSV
write.table(YourFile, "yourfile.txt", quote = FALSE, row.names = TRUE, sep = "\t")

# Tidyverse version
write_csv(YourFile, "yourfile.csv")



header = TRUE indicates the first row contains column names
row.names = 1 indicates the first column contains row names
sep specifies the delimiter (, for CSV, \t for TSV)
The tidyverse read_csv() and read_tsv() functions return tibbles (enhanced data frames)

Indexing Data Frames

OutputCodeInterpretation

         hydrogel_concentration compression conductivity

# Access by column number
print(mydata[, 2])

# Access by column name (preferred)

[row, column] notation accesses specific elements
$ notation accesses columns by name (preferred — more readable)

Logical conditions return TRUE/FALSE vectors for subsetting

plot(seq_1,

     type = "p",        # "p" = points, "l" = lines, "b" = both
     col = "red",

plot() is the main high-level plotting function in base R
Arguments like xlab, ylab, col, and type control appearance

plot(seq_2, xlab = "time", ylab = "p in pop 2", type = "p", col = 'green')

title: Appendix D - R Programming Fundamentals
text:
Tidy Data Principles







Rules of Thumb for Data Organization



Store data in nonproprietary formats (plain ASCII text / flat files)
Leave an uncorrected original file when doing analyses
Use descriptive names for your data files and variables
Include a header line with descriptive variable names
Maintain effective metadata about the data (data dictionary)
When you add observations, add rows
When you add variables, add columns
A column of data should contain only one data type
